|21||[[1970年]]5月19日||[[福島県]]（1）||51||[[2000年]][[4月23日]]||大分県（2） |- |22||[[1971年]][[4月18日]]||[[島根県]]（1）
[[広島県]]（1）||52||[[2001年]]5月20日||山梨県（2） |- |23||[[1972年]][[5月21日]]||[[新潟県]]（1）||53||[[2002年]][[6月2日]]||山形県（2） |- |24||[[1973年]]4月8日||[[宮崎県]]（1）||54||[[2003年]]5月18日||千葉県（2） |- |25||[[1974年]]5月19日||[[岩手県]]（1）||55||[[2004年]]4月25日||宮崎県（2） |- |26||[[1975年]][[5月25日]]||[[滋賀県]]（1）||56||[[2005年]][[6月5日]]||茨城県（2） |- |27||[[1976年]][[5月23日]]||[[茨城県]]（1）||57||[[2006年]]5月21日||岐阜県（2） |- |28||[[1977年]]4月17日||[[和歌山県]]（1）||58||[[2007年]][[6月24日]]||北海道（2） |- |29||[[1978年]][[5月21日]]||[[高知県]]（1）||59||[[2008年]][[6月15日]]||秋田県（2） |- |30||[[1979年]][[5月27日]]||[[愛知県]]（1）||60||[[2009年]][[6月7日]]||福井県（2） |} == 行幸啓が中止された際の措置 == 1968年（昭和43年）5月19日、秋田県で開催された第19回植樹祭では、3日前に[[十勝沖地震 (1968年)|十勝沖地震]]が発生し行幸啓が中止となった。秋田県側は、急遽、秋田[[スギ]]苗木と鉢を[[皇居]]へ持ち込み「お手植え」をしてもらい会場へ搬送、当日に植栽箇所へ改めて移植するという手段を採った。「お言葉」は[[徳川義寛]][[侍従]]が代読している{{Cite book |和書 |author=社団法人国土緑化推進委員会 |year=1970 |title=国土緑化20年の歩み |page=p322 |publisher=社団法人国土緑化推進委員会 }}{{Cite web|和書|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210602213127/https://www.jiji.com/amp/article?k=2021052900154&g=soc|title=３０日に２年ぶり植樹祭　天皇陛下、リモートでお言葉―過去にも訪問見送り|website=[[時事通信社#ポータルサイト『時事ドットコム』|JIJI.COM]]|work=[[時事通信社]]|date=2021-05-29|accessdate=2021-05-30}}。 なお、開催地が2巡目となり、再び秋田県に開催地がめぐってきた2008年（平成20年）には、開催前日に[[岩手・宮城内陸地震]]が発生。開催自体が危ぶまれたが、翌日、11,500人の出席者を得て開催。「お手植え」、「お手撒き」も実施された{{Cite web|和書|author= |date=2016-08-03 |url=http://www.pref.akita.lg.jp/www/genre/0000000000000/1143838524098/index.html |title=第59回全国植樹祭（森林整備課ホームページ内） |work= |publisher=秋田県 |accessdate=2016-08-06}}。 2020年（令和2年）に予定していた第71回（島根大会）は新型コロナ感染拡大のため1年延期となり、翌2021年（令和3年）5月30日に開催されたが、感染状況を考慮して行幸啓は行わず、[[東宮御所|赤坂御所]]（[[赤坂御用地]]）と島根県内の会場を中継でつなぎ、「お手植え」「お手撒き」を東京で行った後、後日、会場まで運搬されて移し替えられた{{R|:j}}。翌年も同様。以降予定していた大会も1年ずつ後ろ倒しされた{{Cite web|和書|date=2020-04-06 |url= https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO57715970W0A400C2000000/|title=全国植樹祭、1年延期　島根県で開催予定 |publisher=日本経済新聞 |accessdate=2020-04-29}}。 == 全国緑化行事発祥の地 == *1934年、第1回愛林日では茨城県[[筑波山]]麓の鬼ヶ作国有林（現：茨城県[[桜川市]][[真壁町]]大字羽鳥）にて植樹行事が行われた。この場所は、長らく忘れ去られていたが後年になり特定、全国緑化行事発祥の地として2013年度に[[林業遺産]]（No.3）に認定。石碑が建立されている{{Cite web|和書|date= |url=https://www.forestry.jp/activity/forestrylegacy/catalog/2013no3.html |title=2013年度林業遺産（No.3） 全国緑化行事発祥の地 |publisher=日本森林学会 |accessdate=2020-02-01}}。 == 昭和天皇のお手植え == * [[1947年]][[10月30日]]から[[11月2日]]にかけて[[昭和天皇の戦後巡幸]]が富山県内で行われた{{Cite book |和書 |author=原武史 |title=昭和天皇御召列車全記録 |publisher=新潮社 |year=2016-09-30 |page=98|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}}。その際、天皇は[[林業]]に関心を示し、富山県[[細入村]]（現：[[富山市]]西笹津）の[[高山本線]]沿いの斜面で[[タテヤマスギ]]の植樹を行った。それまで天皇による公式行事での植樹は、枯死した際に責任が関係者に及ぶことを危惧し忌避されていたが、「そんな時代でもないであろう」ということとなり、全国で初めて行われることとなった。これが契機となり全国植樹祭への出席、「お手植え」に結び付いている。その後、[[1958年]]10月に開催された[[第13回国民体育大会|富山国体]]、[[1969年]]5月に開催された全国植樹祭で富山県に再び行幸した際には、高山本線で運行された[[お召列車]]の車窓から自ら植えたスギを眺めている北日本新聞2017年5月28日朝刊6面。この場所は、2019年現在も地域住民ら有志の手により管理が行われている{{Cite web|和書|date=2019-05-17 |url=https://www.hokkoku.co.jp/subpage/T20190517204.htm |title=富山の「お手植え杉」美しく　県神社庁と地域住民清掃 |publisher=北国新聞 |accessdate=2020-02-01}}。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === {{Notelist2}} === 出典 === {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == {{Div col}} * [[全国豊かな海づくり大会]] * [[みどりの日]] * [[全国都市緑化フェア]] * [[御野立所]] {{Div col end}} == 外部リンク == * [https://www.rinya.maff.go.jp/j/ryokka/syokuju/index.html 林野庁による全国植樹祭紹介] {{Plant-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:せんこくしよくしゆさい}} [[Category:日本の森林に関する祭事]] [[Category:1950年開始のイベント]]

revision.text:
'''全国植樹祭'''（ぜんこくしょくじゅさい）は、日本で行われる[[植樹祭]]。[[国土緑化運動]]の中核的な行事として、[[1950年]]（[[昭和]]25年）以降、毎年[[春]]に開催されている。 == 概要 == [[ファイル:Saitobaru Onodatesho 2004.JPG|thumb|第55回会場・[[西都原古墳群]]の「お野立所」]] [[国土緑化推進機構]]と開催[[都道府県]]（全都道府県の持ち回り）が主催している。大会会長は[[衆議院議長]]。 大会式典では、[[天皇]]の「[[おことば]]」、天皇・[[皇后]]による「お手植え・お手まき」行事、県内外の参加者による記念植樹、国土緑化運動ポスターコンクール等の表彰行事、大会宣言が行われることが恒例となっており、[[国民スポーツ大会]]・[[全国豊かな海づくり大会]]・[[国民文化祭]]と並び「四大[[行幸]]啓」の一つに位置付けられている。大会は後日NHKで編集版が放送される。 大会の前身は、[[第二次世界大戦]]以前から行われていた「[[愛林日]]植樹行事」に遡る。天皇の行幸は、[[1949年]]（昭和24年）に[[神奈川県]][[箱根町]][[仙石原]]で行われた愛林日記念植樹式から始められている{{Cite book |和書 |author=原武史 |title=昭和天皇御召列車全記録 |publisher=新潮社 |year=2016-09-30 |page=101 |isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=国土緑化運動十五年 |year=1965 |publisher=国土緑化推進委員会 |page=110 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/2506203/1/63}}。現在の植樹祭につながるものは、1950年（昭和25年）に山梨県で「植樹行事並びに国土緑化大会」として開催されたものである。 開催日は、「緑の週間」に合わせて4月第1週（当時）に設定されていた「両陛下、植樹祭へ」『朝日新聞』昭和26年3月31日が、[[1960年]]（昭和35年）の第11回開催以降は5月に設定されることが増えた。 [[1970年]]（昭和45年）の第21回（[[福島県]]）から現在の名称になり、今日に至る。[[1977年]]（昭和52年）の第28回（[[和歌山県]]）からは、秋に過去の植樹祭での手植え・手まきにより成長した木の手入れ（枝払いなど）を行う'''[[全国育樹祭]]'''が行われており、歴代の皇太子（皇嗣）が出席している。 参加者数は、最盛期には1万人を超える規模になり、[[2002年]]（平成14年）に山形県[[金山町 (山形県)|金山町]]で開催された会場では12,000人を数えたが、2000年代に入ると地方自治体の資金難や広い植樹会場を設営することが困難などの理由で縮小傾向となり、[[2013年]]（平成25年）の鳥取県[[南部町 (鳥取県)|南部町]]の会場ではスタッフを含めて7,000人規模となっている[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/656620/jissigaiyou1.pdf 2013年全国植樹祭の概要（鳥取県ホームページ）]。 [[2009年]]（平成27年）の第60回（[[福井県]]）からは当時の天皇[[明仁]]（現・上皇）の公務負担軽減策の一環として植樹祭式典での「おことば」は取りやめになり、平成年間中はこのまま継続した。[[徳仁]]の皇位継承後、2019年（令和元年）の第70回大会（[[愛知県]]）において、天皇の「おことば」が復活した{{Cite news | author=中田絢子 | url =https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM5X5DNLM5XUTIL04S.html | title =天皇のおことば、１１年ぶりに復活　両陛下が植樹祭出席 | newspaper =[[朝日新聞]] | date=2019-6-2 | accessdate =2019-6-2}}{{Cite news | author= | url =https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO45589990S9A600C1000000/ | title =全国植樹祭での天皇陛下のお言葉全文 | newspaper =[[日本経済新聞]] | date=2019-6-2 | accessdate =2019-6-2}}。 == 大会の歴史 == ※（）内…開催回数。 {| class="wikitable" |19||[[1968年]][[5月19日]]||[[秋田県]]（1）||49||[[1998年]]5月10日||群馬県（2） |- |20||[[1969年]][[5月26日]]||[[富山県]]（1）||50||[[1999年]][[5月30日]]||静岡県（2） |- [[広島県]]（1）||52||[[2001年]]5月20日||山梨県（2） |- |23||[[1972年]][[5月21日]]||[[新潟県]]（1）||53||[[2002年]][[6月2日]]||山形県（2） |- |24||[[1973年]]4月8日||[[宮崎県]]（1）||54||[[2003年]]5月18日||千葉県（2） |- |25||[[1974年]]5月19日||[[岩手県]]（1）||55||[[2004年]]4月25日||宮崎県（2） |- |26||[[1975年]][[5月25日]]||[[滋賀県]]（1）||56||[[2005年]][[6月5日]]||茨城県（2） |- |27||[[1976年]][[5月23日]]||[[茨城県]]（1）||57||[[2006年]]5月21日||岐阜県（2） |- |28||[[1977年]]4月17日||[[和歌山県]]（1）||58||[[2007年]][[6月24日]]||北海道（2） |- |29||[[1978年]][[5月21日]]||[[高知県]]（1）||59||[[2008年]][[6月15日]]||秋田県（2） |- |30||[[1979年]][[5月27日]]||[[愛知県]]（1）||60||[[2009年]][[6月7日]]||福井県（2） |} == 行幸啓が中止された際の措置 == 1968年（昭和43年）5月19日、秋田県で開催された第19回植樹祭では、3日前に[[十勝沖地震 (1968年)|十勝沖地震]]が発生し行幸啓が中止となった。秋田県側は、急遽、秋田[[スギ]]苗木と鉢を[[皇居]]へ持ち込み「お手植え」をしてもらい会場へ搬送、当日に植栽箇所へ改めて移植するという手段を採った。「お言葉」は[[徳川義寛]][[侍従]]が代読している{{Cite book |和書 |author=社団法人国土緑化推進委員会 |year=1970 |title=国土緑化20年の歩み |page=p322 |publisher=社団法人国土緑化推進委員会 }}{{Cite web|和書|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210602213127/https://www.jiji.com/amp/article?k=2021052900154&g=soc|title=３０日に２年ぶり植樹祭　天皇陛下、リモートでお言葉―過去にも訪問見送り|website=[[時事通信社#ポータルサイト『時事ドットコム』|JIJI.COM]]|work=[[時事通信社]]|date=2021-05-29|accessdate=2021-05-30}}。 なお、開催地が2巡目となり、再び秋田県に開催地がめぐってきた2008年（平成20年）には、開催前日に[[岩手・宮城内陸地震]]が発生。開催自体が危ぶまれたが、翌日、11,500人の出席者を得て開催。「お手植え」、「お手撒き」も実施された{{Cite web|和書|author= |date=2016-08-03 |url=http://www.pref.akita.lg.jp/www/genre/0000000000000/1143838524098/index.html |title=第59回全国植樹祭（森林整備課ホームページ内） |work= |publisher=秋田県 |accessdate=2016-08-06}}。 2020年（令和2年）に予定していた第71回（島根大会）は新型コロナ感染拡大のため1年延期となり、翌2021年（令和3年）5月30日に開催されたが、感染状況を考慮して行幸啓は行わず、[[東宮御所|赤坂御所]]（[[赤坂御用地]]）と島根県内の会場を中継でつなぎ、「お手植え」「お手撒き」を東京で行った後、後日、会場まで運搬されて移し替えられた{{R|:j}}。翌年も同様。以降予定していた大会も1年ずつ後ろ倒しされた{{Cite web|和書|date=2020-04-06 |url= https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO57715970W0A400C2000000/|title=全国植樹祭、1年延期　島根県で開催予定 |publisher=日本経済新聞 |accessdate=2020-04-29}}。 == 全国緑化行事発祥の地 == *1934年、第1回愛林日では茨城県[[筑波山]]麓の鬼ヶ作国有林（現：茨城県[[桜川市]][[真壁町]]大字羽鳥）にて植樹行事が行われた。この場所は、長らく忘れ去られていたが後年になり特定、全国緑化行事発祥の地として2013年度に[[林業遺産]]（No.3）に認定。石碑が建立されている{{Cite web|和書|date= |url=https://www.forestry.jp/activity/forestrylegacy/catalog/2013no3.html |title=2013年度林業遺産（No.3） 全国緑化行事発祥の地 |publisher=日本森林学会 |accessdate=2020-02-01}}。 == 昭和天皇のお手植え == * [[1947年]][[10月30日]]から[[11月2日]]にかけて[[昭和天皇の戦後巡幸]]が富山県内で行われた{{Cite book |和書 |author=原武史 |title=昭和天皇御召列車全記録 |publisher=新潮社 |year=2016-09-30 |page=98|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}}。その際、天皇は[[林業]]に関心を示し、富山県[[細入村]]（現：[[富山市]]西笹津）の[[高山本線]]沿いの斜面で[[タテヤマスギ]]の植樹を行った。それまで天皇による公式行事での植樹は、枯死した際に責任が関係者に及ぶことを危惧し忌避されていたが、「そんな時代でもないであろう」ということとなり、全国で初めて行われることとなった。これが契機となり全国植樹祭への出席、「お手植え」に結び付いている。その後、[[1958年]]10月に開催された[[第13回国民体育大会|富山国体]]、[[1969年]]5月に開催された全国植樹祭で富山県に再び行幸した際には、高山本線で運行された[[お召列車]]の車窓から自ら植えたスギを眺めている北日本新聞2017年5月28日朝刊6面。この場所は、2019年現在も地域住民ら有志の手により管理が行われている{{Cite web|和書|date=2019-05-17 |url=https://www.hokkoku.co.jp/subpage/T20190517204.htm |title=富山の「お手植え杉」美しく　県神社庁と地域住民清掃 |publisher=北国新聞 |accessdate=2020-02-01}}。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === {{Notelist2}} === 出典 === {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == {{Div col}} * [[全国豊かな海づくり大会]] * [[みどりの日]] * [[全国都市緑化フェア]] * [[御野立所]] {{Div col end}} == 外部リンク == * [https://www.rinya.maff.go.jp/j/ryokka/syokuju/index.html 林野庁による全国植樹祭紹介] {{Plant-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:せんこくしよくしゆさい}} [[Category:日本の森林に関する祭事]] [[Category:1950年開始のイベント]]

revision.comment: /* 昭和天皇のお手植え */ 昭和天皇のお手植えの例を追記
revision.text:
'''全国植樹祭'''（ぜんこくしょくじゅさい）は、日本で行われる[[植樹祭]]。[[国土緑化運動]]の中核的な行事として、[[1950年]]（[[昭和]]25年）以降、毎年[[春]]に開催されている。 == 概要 == [[ファイル:Saitobaru Onodatesho 2004.JPG|thumb|第55回会場・[[西都原古墳群]]の「お野立所」]] [[国土緑化推進機構]]と開催[[都道府県]]（全都道府県の持ち回り）が主催している。大会会長は[[衆議院議長]]。 大会式典では、[[天皇]]の「[[おことば]]」、天皇・[[皇后]]による「お手植え・お手まき」行事、県内外の参加者による記念植樹、国土緑化運動ポスターコンクール等の表彰行事、大会宣言が行われることが恒例となっており、[[国民スポーツ大会]]・[[全国豊かな海づくり大会]]・[[国民文化祭]]と並び「四大[[行幸]]啓」の一つに位置付けられている。大会は後日NHKで編集版が放送される。 大会の前身は、[[第二次世界大戦]]以前から行われていた「[[愛林日]]植樹行事」に遡る。天皇の行幸は、[[1949年]]（昭和24年）に[[神奈川県]][[箱根町]][[仙石原]]で行われた愛林日記念植樹式から始められている{{Cite book |和書 |author=原武史 |title=昭和天皇御召列車全記録 |publisher=新潮社 |year=2016-09-30 |page=101 |isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}}{{Cite book|和書 |title=国土緑化運動十五年 |year=1965 |publisher=国土緑化推進委員会 |page=110 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/2506203/1/63}}。現在の植樹祭につながるものは、1950年（昭和25年）に山梨県で「植樹行事並びに国土緑化大会」として開催されたものである。 開催日は、「緑の週間」に合わせて4月第1週（当時）に設定されていた「両陛下、植樹祭へ」『朝日新聞』昭和26年3月31日が、[[1960年]]（昭和35年）の第11回開催以降は5月に設定されることが増えた。 [[1970年]]（昭和45年）の第21回（[[福島県]]）から現在の名称になり、今日に至る。[[1977年]]（昭和52年）の第28回（[[和歌山県]]）からは、秋に過去の植樹祭での手植え・手まきにより成長した木の手入れ（枝払いなど）を行う'''[[全国育樹祭]]'''が行われており、歴代の皇太子（皇嗣）が出席している。 参加者数は、最盛期には1万人を超える規模になり、[[2002年]]（平成14年）に山形県[[金山町 (山形県)|金山町]]で開催された会場では12,000人を数えたが、2000年代に入ると地方自治体の資金難や広い植樹会場を設営することが困難などの理由で縮小傾向となり、[[2013年]]（平成25年）の鳥取県[[南部町 (鳥取県)|南部町]]の会場ではスタッフを含めて7,000人規模となっている[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/656620/jissigaiyou1.pdf 2013年全国植樹祭の概要（鳥取県ホームページ）]。 [[2009年]]（平成27年）の第60回（[[福井県]]）からは当時の天皇[[明仁]]（現・上皇）の公務負担軽減策の一環として植樹祭式典での「おことば」は取りやめになり、平成年間中はこのまま継続した。[[徳仁]]の皇位継承後、2019年（令和元年）の第70回大会（[[愛知県]]）において、天皇の「おことば」が復活した{{Cite news | author=中田絢子 | url =https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM5X5DNLM5XUTIL04S.html | title =天皇のおことば、１１年ぶりに復活　両陛下が植樹祭出席 | newspaper =[[朝日新聞]] | date=2019-6-2 | accessdate =2019-6-2}}{{Cite news | author= | url =https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO45589990S9A600C1000000/ | title =全国植樹祭での天皇陛下のお言葉全文 | newspaper =[[日本経済新聞]] | date=2019-6-2 | accessdate =2019-6-2}}。 == 大会の歴史 == ※（）内…開催回数。 {| class="wikitable" なお、開催地が2巡目となり、再び秋田県に開催地がめぐってきた2008年（平成20年）には、開催前日に[[岩手・宮城内陸地震]]が発生。開催自体が危ぶまれたが、翌日、11,500人の出席者を得て開催。「お手植え」、「お手撒き」も実施された{{Cite web|和書|author= |date=2016-08-03 |url=http://www.pref.akita.lg.jp/www/genre/0000000000000/1143838524098/index.html |title=第59回全国植樹祭（森林整備課ホームページ内） |work= |publisher=秋田県 |accessdate=2016-08-06}}。 2020年（令和2年）に予定していた第71回（島根大会）は新型コロナ感染拡大のため1年延期となり、翌2021年（令和3年）5月30日に開催されたが、感染状況を考慮して行幸啓は行わず、[[東宮御所|赤坂御所]]（[[赤坂御用地]]）と島根県内の会場を中継でつなぎ、「お手植え」「お手撒き」を東京で行った後、後日、会場まで運搬されて移し替えられた{{R|:j}}。翌年も同様。以降予定していた大会も1年ずつ後ろ倒しされた{{Cite web|和書|date=2020-04-06 |url= https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO57715970W0A400C2000000/|title=全国植樹祭、1年延期　島根県で開催予定 |publisher=日本経済新聞 |accessdate=2020-04-29}}。 == 全国緑化行事発祥の地 == *1934年、第1回愛林日では茨城県[[筑波山]]麓の鬼ヶ作国有林（現：茨城県[[桜川市]][[真壁町]]大字羽鳥）にて植樹行事が行われた。この場所は、長らく忘れ去られていたが後年になり特定、全国緑化行事発祥の地として2013年度に[[林業遺産]]（No.3）に認定。石碑が建立されている{{Cite web|和書|date= |url=https://www.forestry.jp/activity/forestrylegacy/catalog/2013no3.html |title=2013年度林業遺産（No.3） 全国緑化行事発祥の地 |publisher=日本森林学会 |accessdate=2020-02-01}}。 == 昭和天皇のお手植え == [[1947年]][[10月30日]]から[[11月2日]]にかけて[[昭和天皇の戦後巡幸]]が富山県内で行われた{{Cite book |和書 |author=原武史 |title=昭和天皇御召列車全記録 |publisher=新潮社 |year=2016-09-30 |page=98|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}}。その際、天皇は[[林業]]に関心を示し、富山県[[細入村]]（現：[[富山市]]西笹津）の[[高山本線]]沿いの斜面で[[タテヤマスギ]]の植樹を行った。それまで天皇による公式行事での植樹は、枯死した際に責任が関係者に及ぶことを危惧し忌避されていた昭和天皇が[[摂政]]時代に[[宮城県]]の[[瑞巌寺]]で[[アスナロ]]を植樹した例などが僅かにある（{{Cite book |和書 |author=宮内庁 |title=昭和天皇実録第十 |publisher=東京書籍 |year=2017-03-30 |page=394 |isbn=978-4-487-74410-7 }}）が、「そんな時代でもないであろう」ということとなり、全国で初めて行われることとなった。これが契機となり全国植樹祭への出席、「お手植え」に結び付いている。その後、[[1958年]]10月に開催された[[第13回国民体育大会|富山国体]]、[[1969年]]5月に開催された全国植樹祭で富山県に再び行幸した際には、高山本線で運行された[[お召列車]]の車窓から自ら植えたスギを眺めている北日本新聞2017年5月28日朝刊6面。この場所は、2019年現在も地域住民ら有志の手により管理が行われている{{Cite web|和書|date=2019-05-17 |url=https://www.hokkoku.co.jp/subpage/T20190517204.htm |title=富山の「お手植え杉」美しく　県神社庁と地域住民清掃 |publisher=北国新聞 |accessdate=2020-02-01}}。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === {{Notelist2}} === 出典 === {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == {{Div col}} * [[全国豊かな海づくり大会]] * [[みどりの日]] * [[全国都市緑化フェア]] * [[御野立所]] {{Div col end}} == 外部リンク == * [https://www.rinya.maff.go.jp/j/ryokka/syokuju/index.html 林野庁による全国植樹祭紹介] {{Plant-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:せんこくしよくしゆさい}} [[Category:日本の森林に関する祭事]] [[Category:1950年開始のイベント]]

[[category:new pages]][[ja:全国植樹祭]] '''National Tree-Planting Festival''' (全国植樹祭, Zenkoku Shokujusai) is a Japanese tree-planting festival. As a central event of the {{ill|National Land Afforestation Campaign|ja|国土緑化運動}}, it has been held annually in spring since 1950 (Shōwa 25).
== Overview == [[File:Saitobaru Onodatesho 2004.JPG|thumb|Venue for the 55th festival at {{ill|Saitobaru Burial Mounds|ja|西都原古墳群}}, featuring the "Onodate Place"]] The festival is organized by the {{ill|National Land Afforestation Promotion Organization|ja|国土緑化推進機構}} and the host prefecture (rotating among all prefectures). The president of the event is traditionally the Speaker of the House of Representatives.
During the main ceremony, the Emperor delivers an "Imperial Address" (おことば), participates in the ceremonial tree planting and seed sowing alongside the Empress, and attends memorial tree planting events by participants from within and outside the host prefecture. The ceremony also features awards for contests such as the National Land Afforestation Poster Competition and culminates with a festival declaration. The event is recognized as one of the "Four Major Imperial Visits," along with the {{ill|National Sports Festival|ja|国民スポーツ大会}}, {{ill|National Festival of the Sea|ja|全国豊かな海づくり大会}}, and the {{ill|National Cultural Festival|ja|国民文化祭}}. Highlights of the event are later broadcast in an edited format by NHK.
The festival traces its roots to the "Forest Day Planting Ceremony" held before World War II. The Emperor's involvement began with a commemorative tree-planting ceremony held in 1949 (Shōwa 24) at {{ill|Sengokuhara|ja|仙石原}} in Hakone Town, Kanagawa Prefecture.<ref>{{Cite book |language=ja |author=Takeshi Hara |title=Complete Record of Emperor Shōwa's Imperial Train Journeys |publisher=Shinchōsha |year=2016 |page=101 |isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |language=ja |title=Fifteen Years of the National Land Afforestation Movement |year=1965 |publisher=National Land Afforestation Promotion Committee |page=110 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/2506203/1/63}}</ref> The event evolved into its current form starting with the "Tree-Planting Ceremony and National Land Afforestation Festival" held in Yamanashi Prefecture in 1950 (Shōwa 25).
Initially scheduled in the first week of April to coincide with "Green Week,"<ref>“Their Majesties Attend Tree-Planting Festival,” *Asahi Shimbun,* March 31, 1951</ref> the festival has increasingly been held in May since the 11th event in 1960 (Shōwa 35).
In 1970 (Shōwa 45), during the 21st festival in Fukushima Prefecture, the event adopted its current name. From 1977 (Shōwa 52), starting with the 28th festival in Wakayama Prefecture, the {{ill|National Tree-Care Festival|ja|全国育樹祭}} has been held in autumn to prune and care for trees planted during previous festivals, with the Crown Prince attending these events.
At its peak, the festival drew over 10,000 participants. For instance, the venue in Kaneyama Town, Yamagata Prefecture, in 2002 (Heisei 14) attracted 12,000 attendees. However, since the 2000s, financial difficulties faced by local governments and challenges in setting up large venues have led to a decline in scale, with the 2013 (Heisei 25) venue in Nanbu Town, Tottori Prefecture, accommodating around 7,000 people, including staff.<ref>[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/656620/jissigaiyou1.pdf Overview of the 2013 National Tree-Planting Festival (Tottori Prefecture website)]</ref>
Starting with the 60th festival in 2009 (Heisei 21) in Fukui Prefecture, the Emperor's "Imperial Address" was omitted as part of efforts to reduce the public duties of Emperor Akihito (current Emperor Emeritus), and this practice continued throughout the Heisei era. Following the accession of Emperor Naruhito, the "Imperial Address" was reinstated during the 70th festival in 2019 (Reiwa 1) in Aichi Prefecture.<ref>{{Cite news |author=Ayako Nakata |url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM5X5DNLM5XUTIL04S.html |title=Imperial Address Resumes at National Tree-Planting Festival After 11 Years |newspaper=Asahi Shimbun |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO45589990S9A600C1000000/ |title=Full Text of Emperor’s Address at National Tree-Planting Festival |newspaper=Nikkei |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref>

Starting with the 60th festival in 2009 (Heisei 21) in Fukui Prefecture, the Emperor's "Imperial Address" was omitted as part of efforts to reduce the public duties of Emperor Akihito (current Emperor Emeritus), and this practice continued throughout the Heisei era. Following the accession of Emperor Naruhito, the "Imperial Address" was reinstated during the 70th festival in 2019 (Reiwa 1) in Aichi Prefecture.<ref>{{Cite news |author=Ayako Nakata |url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM5X5DNLM5XUTIL04S.html |title=Imperial Address Resumes at National Tree-Planting Festival After 11 Years |newspaper=Asahi Shimbun |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO45589990S9A600C1000000/ |title=Full Text of Emperor’s Address at National Tree-Planting Festival |newspaper=Nikkei |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref>

Starting with the 60th festival in 2009 (Heisei 21) in Fukui Prefecture, the Emperor's "Imperial Address" was omitted as part of efforts to reduce the public duties of Emperor Akihito (current Emperor Emeritus), and this practice continued throughout the Heisei era. Following the accession of Emperor Naruhito, the "Imperial Address" was reinstated during the 70th festival in 2019 (Reiwa 1) in Aichi Prefecture.<ref>{{Cite news |author=Ayako Nakata |url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM5X5DNLM5XUTIL04S.html |title=Imperial Address Resumes at National Tree-Planting Festival After 11 Years |newspaper=Asahi Shimbun |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO45589990S9A600C1000000/ |title=Full Text of Emperor’s Address at National Tree-Planting Festival |newspaper=Nikkei |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref>

[[category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:new pages]][[Category:Recurring events established in 1950]] [[Category:new pages]][[category:new pages]][[ja:全国植樹祭]] '''National Tree-Planting Festival''' (全国植樹祭, Zenkoku Shokujusai) is a Japanese tree-planting festival. As a central event of the {{ill|National Land Afforestation Campaign|ja|国土緑化運動}}, it has been held annually in spring since 1950 (Shōwa 25).
== Overview == [[File:Saitobaru Onodatesho 2004.JPG|thumb|Venue for the 55th festival at {{ill|Saitobaru Burial Mounds|ja|西都原古墳群}}, featuring the "Onodate Place"]] The festival is organized by the {{ill|National Land Afforestation Promotion Organization|ja|国土緑化推進機構}} and the host prefecture (rotating among all prefectures). The president of the event is traditionally the Speaker of the House of Representatives.
During the main ceremony, the Emperor delivers an "Imperial Address" (おことば), participates in the ceremonial tree planting and seed sowing alongside the Empress, and attends memorial tree planting events by participants from within and outside the host prefecture. The ceremony also features awards for contests such as the National Land Afforestation Poster Competition and culminates with a festival declaration. The event is recognized as one of the "Four Major Imperial Visits," along with the {{ill|National Sports Festival|ja|国民スポーツ大会}}, {{ill|National Festival of the Sea|ja|全国豊かな海づくり大会}}, and the {{ill|National Cultural Festival|ja|国民文化祭}}. Highlights of the event are later broadcast in an edited format by NHK.
The festival traces its roots to the "Forest Day Planting Ceremony" held before World War II. The Emperor's involvement began with a commemorative tree-planting ceremony held in 1949 (Shōwa 24) at {{ill|Sengokuhara|ja|仙石原}} in Hakone Town, Kanagawa Prefecture.<ref>{{Cite book |language=ja |author=Takeshi Hara |title=Complete Record of Emperor Shōwa's Imperial Train Journeys |publisher=Shinchōsha |year=2016 |page=101 |isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |language=ja |title=Fifteen Years of the National Land Afforestation Movement |year=1965 |publisher=National Land Afforestation Promotion Committee |page=110 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/2506203/1/63}}</ref> The event evolved into its current form starting with the "Tree-Planting Ceremony and National Land Afforestation Festival" held in Yamanashi Prefecture in 1950 (Shōwa 25).

From 1977 (Shōwa 52), starting with the 28th festival in Wakayama Prefecture, the {{ill|National Tree-Care Festival|ja|全国育樹祭}} has been held in autumn to prune and care for trees planted during previous festivals, with the Crown Prince attending these events.

However, since the 2000s, financial difficulties faced by local governments and challenges in setting up large venues have led to a decline in scale, with the 2013 (Heisei 25) venue in Nanbu Town, Tottori Prefecture, accommodating around 7,000 people, including staff.<ref>[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/656620/jissigaiyou1.pdf Overview of the 2013 National Tree-Planting Festival (Tottori Prefecture website)]</ref>

Following the accession of Emperor Naruhito, the "Imperial Address" was reinstated during the 70th festival in 2019 (Reiwa 1) in Aichi Prefecture.<ref>{{Cite news |author=Ayako Nakata |url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM5X5DNLM5XUTIL04S.html |title=Imperial Address Resumes at National Tree-Planting Festival After 11 Years |newspaper=Asahi Shimbun |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO45589990S9A600C1000000/ |title=Full Text of Emperor’s Address at National Tree-Planting Festival |newspaper=Nikkei |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref>

In 1970 (Shōwa 45), during the 21st festival in Fukushima Prefecture, the event adopted its current name. From 1977 (Shōwa 52), starting with the 28th festival in Wakayama Prefecture, the {{ill|National Tree-Care Festival|ja|全国育樹祭}} has been held in autumn to prune and care for trees planted during previous festivals, with the Crown Prince attending these events.
At its peak, the festival drew over 10,000 participants. For instance, the venue in Kaneyama Town, Yamagata Prefecture, in 2002 (Heisei 14) attracted 12,000 attendees. However, since the 2000s, financial difficulties faced by local governments and challenges in setting up large venues have led to a decline in scale, with the 2013 (Heisei 25) venue in Nanbu Town, Tottori Prefecture, accommodating around 7,000 people, including staff.<ref>[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/656620/jissigaiyou1.pdf Overview of the 2013 National Tree-Planting Festival (Tottori Prefecture website)]</ref>
Starting with the 60th festival in 2009 (Heisei 21) in Fukui Prefecture, the Emperor's "Imperial Address" was omitted as part of efforts to reduce the public duties of Emperor Akihito (current Emperor Emeritus), and this practice continued throughout the Heisei era. Following the accession of Emperor Naruhito, the "Imperial Address" was reinstated during the 70th festival in 2019 (Reiwa 1) in Aichi Prefecture.<ref>{{Cite news |author=Ayako Nakata |url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM5X5DNLM5XUTIL04S.html |title=Imperial Address Resumes at National Tree-Planting Festival After 11 Years |newspaper=Asahi Shimbun |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO45589990S9A600C1000000/ |title=Full Text of Emperor’s Address at National Tree-Planting Festival |newspaper=Nikkei |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref>

[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:National Tree-Planting Festival]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:National Tree-Planting Festival]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:National Tree-Planting Festival]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Recurring events established in 1950]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:National Tree-Planting Festival]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:National Tree-Planting Festival]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:National Tree-Planting Festival]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:全国植樹祭]] '''National Tree-Planting Festival''' (全国植樹祭, Zenkoku Shokujusai) is a Japanese tree-planting festival. As a central event of the {{ill|National Land Afforestation Campaign|ja|国土緑化運動}}, it has been held annually in spring since 1950 (Shōwa 25).
== Overview == [[File:Saitobaru Onodatesho 2004.JPG|thumb|Venue for the 55th festival at {{ill|Saitobaru Burial Mounds|ja|西都原古墳群}}, featuring the "Onodate Place"]] The festival is organized by the {{ill|National Land Afforestation Promotion Organization|ja|国土緑化推進機構}} and the host prefecture (rotating among all prefectures). The president of the event is traditionally the Speaker of the House of Representatives.
During the main ceremony, the Emperor delivers an "Imperial Address" (おことば), participates in the ceremonial tree planting and seed sowing alongside the Empress, and attends memorial tree planting events by participants from within and outside the host prefecture. The ceremony also features awards for contests such as the National Land Afforestation Poster Competition and culminates with a festival declaration. The event is recognized as one of the "Four Major Imperial Visits," along with the {{ill|National Sports Festival|ja|国民スポーツ大会}}, {{ill|National Festival of the Sea|ja|全国豊かな海づくり大会}}, and the {{ill|National Cultural Festival|ja|国民文化祭}}. Highlights of the event are later broadcast in an edited format by NHK.
The festival traces its roots to the "Forest Day Planting Ceremony" held before World War II. The Emperor's involvement began with a commemorative tree-planting ceremony held in 1949 (Shōwa 24) at {{ill|Sengokuhara|ja|仙石原}} in Hakone Town, Kanagawa Prefecture.<ref>{{Cite book |language=ja |author=Takeshi Hara |title=Complete Record of Emperor Shōwa's Imperial Train Journeys |publisher=Shinchōsha |year=2016 |page=101 |isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |language=ja |title=Fifteen Years of the National Land Afforestation Movement |year=1965 |publisher=National Land Afforestation Promotion Committee |page=110 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/2506203/1/63}}</ref> The event evolved into its current form starting with the "Tree-Planting Ceremony and National Land Afforestation Festival" held in Yamanashi Prefecture in 1950 (Shōwa 25).
Initially scheduled in the first week of April to coincide with "Green Week,"<ref>“Their Majesties Attend Tree-Planting Festival,” *Asahi Shimbun,* March 31, 1951</ref> the festival has increasingly been held in May since the 11th event in 1960 (Shōwa 35).
In 1970 (Shōwa 45), during the 21st festival in Fukushima Prefecture, the event adopted its current name. From 1977 (Shōwa 52), starting with the 28th festival in Wakayama Prefecture, the {{ill|National Tree-Care Festival|ja|全国育樹祭}} has been held in autumn to prune and care for trees planted during previous festivals, with the Crown Prince attending these events.
At its peak, the festival drew over 10,000 participants. For instance, the venue in Kaneyama Town, Yamagata Prefecture, in 2002 (Heisei 14) attracted 12,000 attendees. However, since the 2000s, financial difficulties faced by local governments and challenges in setting up large venues have led to a decline in scale, with the 2013 (Heisei 25) venue in Nanbu Town, Tottori Prefecture, accommodating around 7,000 people, including staff.<ref>[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/656620/jissigaiyou1.pdf Overview of the 2013 National Tree-Planting Festival (Tottori Prefecture website)]</ref>
Starting with the 60th festival in 2009 (Heisei 21) in Fukui Prefecture, the Emperor's "Imperial Address" was omitted as part of efforts to reduce the public duties of Emperor Akihito (current Emperor Emeritus), and this practice continued throughout the Heisei era. Following the accession of Emperor Naruhito, the "Imperial Address" was reinstated during the 70th festival in 2019 (Reiwa 1) in Aichi Prefecture.<ref>{{Cite news |author=Ayako Nakata |url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM5X5DNLM5XUTIL04S.html |title=Imperial Address Resumes at National Tree-Planting Festival After 11 Years |newspaper=Asahi Shimbun |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO45589990S9A600C1000000/ |title=Full Text of Emperor’s Address at National Tree-Planting Festival |newspaper=Nikkei |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref>

As a central event of the {{ill|National Land Afforestation Campaign|ja|国土緑化運動}}, it has been held annually in spring since 1950 (Shōwa 25).

The president of the event is traditionally the Speaker of the House of Representatives.
During the main ceremony, the Emperor delivers an "Imperial Address" (おことば), participates in the ceremonial tree planting and seed sowing alongside the Empress, and attends memorial tree planting events by participants from within and outside the host prefecture. The ceremony also features awards for contests such as the National Land Afforestation Poster Competition and culminates with a festival declaration. The event is recognized as one of the "Four Major Imperial Visits," along with the {{ill|National Sports Festival|ja|国民スポーツ大会}}, {{ill|National Festival of the Sea|ja|全国豊かな海づくり大会}}, and the {{ill|National Cultural Festival|ja|国民文化祭}}. Highlights of the event are later broadcast in an edited format by NHK.
The festival traces its roots to the "Forest Day Planting Ceremony" held before World War II. The Emperor's involvement began with a commemorative tree-planting ceremony held in 1949 (Shōwa 24) at {{ill|Sengokuhara|ja|仙石原}} in Hakone Town, Kanagawa Prefecture.<ref>{{Cite book |language=ja |author=Takeshi Hara |title=Complete Record of Emperor Shōwa's Imperial Train Journeys |publisher=Shinchōsha |year=2016 |page=101 |isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |language=ja |title=Fifteen Years of the National Land Afforestation Movement |year=1965 |publisher=National Land Afforestation Promotion Committee |page=110 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/2506203/1/63}}</ref> The event evolved into its current form starting with the "Tree-Planting Ceremony and National Land Afforestation Festival" held in Yamanashi Prefecture in 1950 (Shōwa 25).
Initially scheduled in the first week of April to coincide with "Green Week,"<ref>“Their Majesties Attend Tree-Planting Festival,” *Asahi Shimbun,* March 31, 1951</ref> the festival has increasingly been held in May since the 11th event in 1960 (Shōwa 35).
In 1970 (Shōwa 45), during the 21st festival in Fukushima Prefecture, the event adopted its current name. From 1977 (Shōwa 52), starting with the 28th festival in Wakayama Prefecture, the {{ill|National Tree-Care Festival|ja|全国育樹祭}} has been held in autumn to prune and care for trees planted during previous festivals, with the Crown Prince attending these events.
At its peak, the festival drew over 10,000 participants. For instance, the venue in Kaneyama Town, Yamagata Prefecture, in 2002 (Heisei 14) attracted 12,000 attendees. However, since the 2000s, financial difficulties faced by local governments and challenges in setting up large venues have led to a decline in scale, with the 2013 (Heisei 25) venue in Nanbu Town, Tottori Prefecture, accommodating around 7,000 people, including staff.<ref>[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/656620/jissigaiyou1.pdf Overview of the 2013 National Tree-Planting Festival (Tottori Prefecture website)]</ref>
Starting with the 60th festival in 2009 (Heisei 21) in Fukui Prefecture, the Emperor's "Imperial Address" was omitted as part of efforts to reduce the public duties of Emperor Akihito (current Emperor Emeritus), and this practice continued throughout the Heisei era. Following the accession of Emperor Naruhito, the "Imperial Address" was reinstated during the 70th festival in 2019 (Reiwa 1) in Aichi Prefecture.<ref>{{Cite news |author=Ayako Nakata |url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM5X5DNLM5XUTIL04S.html |title=Imperial Address Resumes at National Tree-Planting Festival After 11 Years |newspaper=Asahi Shimbun |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO45589990S9A600C1000000/ |title=Full Text of Emperor’s Address at National Tree-Planting Festival |newspaper=Nikkei |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref>

== Overview == [[File:Saitobaru Onodatesho 2004.JPG|thumb|Venue for the 55th festival at {{ill|Saitobaru Burial Mounds|ja|西都原古墳群}}, featuring the "Onodate Place"]] The festival is organized by the {{ill|National Land Afforestation Promotion Organization|ja|国土緑化推進機構}} and the host prefecture (rotating among all prefectures). The president of the event is traditionally the Speaker of the House of Representatives.
During the main ceremony, the Emperor delivers an "Imperial Address" (おことば), participates in the ceremonial tree planting and seed sowing alongside the Empress, and attends memorial tree planting events by participants from within and outside the host prefecture. The ceremony also features awards for contests such as the National Land Afforestation Poster Competition and culminates with a festival declaration. The event is recognized as one of the "Four Major Imperial Visits," along with the {{ill|National Sports Festival|ja|国民スポーツ大会}}, {{ill|National Festival of the Sea|ja|全国豊かな海づくり大会}}, and the {{ill|National Cultural Festival|ja|国民文化祭}}. Highlights of the event are later broadcast in an edited format by NHK.
The festival traces its roots to the "Forest Day Planting Ceremony" held before World War II. The Emperor's involvement began with a commemorative tree-planting ceremony held in 1949 (Shōwa 24) at {{ill|Sengokuhara|ja|仙石原}} in Hakone Town, Kanagawa Prefecture.<ref>{{Cite book |language=ja |author=Takeshi Hara |title=Complete Record of Emperor Shōwa's Imperial Train Journeys |publisher=Shinchōsha |year=2016 |page=101 |isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |language=ja |title=Fifteen Years of the National Land Afforestation Movement |year=1965 |publisher=National Land Afforestation Promotion Committee |page=110 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/2506203/1/63}}</ref> The event evolved into its current form starting with the "Tree-Planting Ceremony and National Land Afforestation Festival" held in Yamanashi Prefecture in 1950 (Shōwa 25).
Initially scheduled in the first week of April to coincide with "Green Week,"<ref>“Their Majesties Attend Tree-Planting Festival,” *Asahi Shimbun,* March 31, 1951</ref> the festival has increasingly been held in May since the 11th event in 1960 (Shōwa 35).
In 1970 (Shōwa 45), during the 21st festival in Fukushima Prefecture, the event adopted its current name. From 1977 (Shōwa 52), starting with the 28th festival in Wakayama Prefecture, the {{ill|National Tree-Care Festival|ja|全国育樹祭}} has been held in autumn to prune and care for trees planted during previous festivals, with the Crown Prince attending these events.
At its peak, the festival drew over 10,000 participants. For instance, the venue in Kaneyama Town, Yamagata Prefecture, in 2002 (Heisei 14) attracted 12,000 attendees. However, since the 2000s, financial difficulties faced by local governments and challenges in setting up large venues have led to a decline in scale, with the 2013 (Heisei 25) venue in Nanbu Town, Tottori Prefecture, accommodating around 7,000 people, including staff.<ref>[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/656620/jissigaiyou1.pdf Overview of the 2013 National Tree-Planting Festival (Tottori Prefecture website)]</ref>
Starting with the 60th festival in 2009 (Heisei 21) in Fukui Prefecture, the Emperor's "Imperial Address" was omitted as part of efforts to reduce the public duties of Emperor Akihito (current Emperor Emeritus), and this practice continued throughout the Heisei era. Following the accession of Emperor Naruhito, the "Imperial Address" was reinstated during the 70th festival in 2019 (Reiwa 1) in Aichi Prefecture.<ref>{{Cite news |author=Ayako Nakata |url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM5X5DNLM5XUTIL04S.html |title=Imperial Address Resumes at National Tree-Planting Festival After 11 Years |newspaper=Asahi Shimbun |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO45589990S9A600C1000000/ |title=Full Text of Emperor’s Address at National Tree-Planting Festival |newspaper=Nikkei |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref>

As a central event of the {{ill|National Land Afforestation Campaign|ja|国土緑化運動}}, it has been held annually in spring since 1950 (Shōwa 25).
== Overview == [[File:Saitobaru Onodatesho 2004.JPG|thumb|Venue for the 55th festival at {{ill|Saitobaru Burial Mounds|ja|西都原古墳群}}, featuring the "Onodate Place"]] The festival is organized by the {{ill|National Land Afforestation Promotion Organization|ja|国土緑化推進機構}} and the host prefecture (rotating among all prefectures). The president of the event is traditionally the Speaker of the House of Representatives.
During the main ceremony, the Emperor delivers an "Imperial Address" (おことば), participates in the ceremonial tree planting and seed sowing alongside the Empress, and attends memorial tree planting events by participants from within and outside the host prefecture. The ceremony also features awards for contests such as the National Land Afforestation Poster Competition and culminates with a festival declaration. The event is recognized as one of the "Four Major Imperial Visits," along with the {{ill|National Sports Festival|ja|国民スポーツ大会}}, {{ill|National Festival of the Sea|ja|全国豊かな海づくり大会}}, and the {{ill|National Cultural Festival|ja|国民文化祭}}. Highlights of the event are later broadcast in an edited format by NHK.
The festival traces its roots to the "Forest Day Planting Ceremony" held before World War II. The Emperor's involvement began with a commemorative tree-planting ceremony held in 1949 (Shōwa 24) at {{ill|Sengokuhara|ja|仙石原}} in Hakone Town, Kanagawa Prefecture.<ref>{{Cite book |language=ja |author=Takeshi Hara |title=Complete Record of Emperor Shōwa's Imperial Train Journeys |publisher=Shinchōsha |year=2016 |page=101 |isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |language=ja |title=Fifteen Years of the National Land Afforestation Movement |year=1965 |publisher=National Land Afforestation Promotion Committee |page=110 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/2506203/1/63}}</ref> The event evolved into its current form starting with the "Tree-Planting Ceremony and National Land Afforestation Festival" held in Yamanashi Prefecture in 1950 (Shōwa 25).
Initially scheduled in the first week of April to coincide with "Green Week,"<ref>“Their Majesties Attend Tree-Planting Festival,” *Asahi Shimbun,* March 31, 1951</ref> the festival has increasingly been held in May since the 11th event in 1960 (Shōwa 35).
In 1970 (Shōwa 45), during the 21st festival in Fukushima Prefecture, the event adopted its current name. From 1977 (Shōwa 52), starting with the 28th festival in Wakayama Prefecture, the {{ill|National Tree-Care Festival|ja|全国育樹祭}} has been held in autumn to prune and care for trees planted during previous festivals, with the Crown Prince attending these events.
At its peak, the festival drew over 10,000 participants. For instance, the venue in Kaneyama Town, Yamagata Prefecture, in 2002 (Heisei 14) attracted 12,000 attendees. However, since the 2000s, financial difficulties faced by local governments and challenges in setting up large venues have led to a decline in scale, with the 2013 (Heisei 25) venue in Nanbu Town, Tottori Prefecture, accommodating around 7,000 people, including staff.<ref>[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/656620/jissigaiyou1.pdf Overview of the 2013 National Tree-Planting Festival (Tottori Prefecture website)]</ref>
Starting with the 60th festival in 2009 (Heisei 21) in Fukui Prefecture, the Emperor's "Imperial Address" was omitted as part of efforts to reduce the public duties of Emperor Akihito (current Emperor Emeritus), and this practice continued throughout the Heisei era. Following the accession of Emperor Naruhito, the "Imperial Address" was reinstated during the 70th festival in 2019 (Reiwa 1) in Aichi Prefecture.<ref>{{Cite news |author=Ayako Nakata |url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM5X5DNLM5XUTIL04S.html |title=Imperial Address Resumes at National Tree-Planting Festival After 11 Years |newspaper=Asahi Shimbun |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO45589990S9A600C1000000/ |title=Full Text of Emperor’s Address at National Tree-Planting Festival |newspaper=Nikkei |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref>

The Emperor's involvement began with a commemorative tree-planting ceremony held in 1949 (Shōwa 24) at {{ill|Sengokuhara|ja|仙石原}} in Hakone Town, Kanagawa Prefecture.<ref>{{Cite book |language=ja |author=Takeshi Hara |title=Complete Record of Emperor Shōwa's Imperial Train Journeys |publisher=Shinchōsha |year=2016 |page=101 |isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |language=ja |title=Fifteen Years of the National Land Afforestation Movement |year=1965 |publisher=National Land Afforestation Promotion Committee |page=110 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/2506203/1/63}}</ref> The event evolved into its current form starting with the "Tree-Planting Ceremony and National Land Afforestation Festival" held in Yamanashi Prefecture in 1950 (Shōwa 25).

However, since the 2000s, financial difficulties faced by local governments and challenges in setting up large venues have led to a decline in scale, with the 2013 (Heisei 25) venue in Nanbu Town, Tottori Prefecture, accommodating around 7,000 people, including staff.<ref>[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/656620/jissigaiyou1.pdf Overview of the 2013 National Tree-Planting Festival (Tottori Prefecture website)]</ref>

[[Category:National Tree-Planting Festival]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-National Tree-Planting Festival-qq]] [[Category:National Tree-Planting Festival]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-National Tree-Planting Festival-qq]] [[Category:National Tree-Planting Festival]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-National Tree-Planting Festival-qq]] [[Category:Recurring events established in 1950]] [[Category:National Tree-Planting Festival]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-National Tree-Planting Festival-qq]] [[Category:National Tree-Planting Festival]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-National Tree-Planting Festival-qq]] [[Category:National Tree-Planting Festival]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-National Tree-Planting Festival-qq]] [[Category:q--q]] [[sn:National Tree-Planting Festival]][[Category:qq]][[sn:National Tree-Planting Festival]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:全国植樹祭]] '''National Tree-Planting Festival''' (全国植樹祭, Zenkoku Shokujusai) is a Japanese tree-planting festival. As a central event of the {{ill|National Land Afforestation Campaign|ja|国土緑化運動}}, it has been held annually in spring since 1950 (Shōwa 25).
== Overview == [[File:Saitobaru Onodatesho 2004.JPG|thumb|Venue for the 55th festival at {{ill|Saitobaru Burial Mounds|ja|西都原古墳群}}, featuring the "Onodate Place"]] The festival is organized by the {{ill|National Land Afforestation Promotion Organization|ja|国土緑化推進機構}} and the host prefecture (rotating among all prefectures). The president of the event is traditionally the Speaker of the House of Representatives.
During the main ceremony, the Emperor delivers an "Imperial Address" (おことば), participates in the ceremonial tree planting and seed sowing alongside the Empress, and attends memorial tree planting events by participants from within and outside the host prefecture. The ceremony also features awards for contests such as the National Land Afforestation Poster Competition and culminates with a festival declaration. The event is recognized as one of the "Four Major Imperial Visits," along with the {{ill|National Sports Festival|ja|国民スポーツ大会}}, {{ill|National Festival of the Sea|ja|全国豊かな海づくり大会}}, and the {{ill|National Cultural Festival|ja|国民文化祭}}. Highlights of the event are later broadcast in an edited format by NHK.
The festival traces its roots to the "Forest Day Planting Ceremony" held before World War II. The Emperor's involvement began with a commemorative tree-planting ceremony held in 1949 (Shōwa 24) at {{ill|Sengokuhara|ja|仙石原}} in Hakone Town, Kanagawa Prefecture.<ref>{{Cite book |language=ja |author=Takeshi Hara |title=Complete Record of Emperor Shōwa's Imperial Train Journeys |publisher=Shinchōsha |year=2016 |page=101 |isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |language=ja |title=Fifteen Years of the National Land Afforestation Movement |year=1965 |publisher=National Land Afforestation Promotion Committee |page=110 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/2506203/1/63}}</ref> The event evolved into its current form starting with the "Tree-Planting Ceremony and National Land Afforestation Festival" held in Yamanashi Prefecture in 1950 (Shōwa 25).
Initially scheduled in the first week of April to coincide with "Green Week,"<ref>“Their Majesties Attend Tree-Planting Festival,” *Asahi Shimbun,* March 31, 1951</ref> the festival has increasingly been held in May since the 11th event in 1960 (Shōwa 35).
In 1970 (Shōwa 45), during the 21st festival in Fukushima Prefecture, the event adopted its current name. From 1977 (Shōwa 52), starting with the 28th festival in Wakayama Prefecture, the {{ill|National Tree-Care Festival|ja|全国育樹祭}} has been held in autumn to prune and care for trees planted during previous festivals, with the Crown Prince attending these events.
At its peak, the festival drew over 10,000 participants. For instance, the venue in Kaneyama Town, Yamagata Prefecture, in 2002 (Heisei 14) attracted 12,000 attendees. However, since the 2000s, financial difficulties faced by local governments and challenges in setting up large venues have led to a decline in scale, with the 2013 (Heisei 25) venue in Nanbu Town, Tottori Prefecture, accommodating around 7,000 people, including staff.<ref>[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/656620/jissigaiyou1.pdf Overview of the 2013 National Tree-Planting Festival (Tottori Prefecture website)]</ref>
Starting with the 60th festival in 2009 (Heisei 21) in Fukui Prefecture, the Emperor's "Imperial Address" was omitted as part of efforts to reduce the public duties of Emperor Akihito (current Emperor Emeritus), and this practice continued throughout the Heisei era. Following the accession of Emperor Naruhito, the "Imperial Address" was reinstated during the 70th festival in 2019 (Reiwa 1) in Aichi Prefecture.<ref>{{Cite news |author=Ayako Nakata |url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM5X5DNLM5XUTIL04S.html |title=Imperial Address Resumes at National Tree-Planting Festival After 11 Years |newspaper=Asahi Shimbun |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO45589990S9A600C1000000/ |title=Full Text of Emperor’s Address at National Tree-Planting Festival |newspaper=Nikkei |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref> [[Category:ppp]]

However, since the 2000s, financial difficulties faced by local governments and challenges in setting up large venues have led to a decline in scale, with the 2013 (Heisei 25) venue in Nanbu Town, Tottori Prefecture, accommodating around 7,000 people, including staff.<ref>[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/656620/jissigaiyou1.pdf Overview of the 2013 National Tree-Planting Festival (Tottori Prefecture website)]</ref>
Starting with the 60th festival in 2009 (Heisei 21) in Fukui Prefecture, the Emperor's "Imperial Address" was omitted as part of efforts to reduce the public duties of Emperor Akihito (current Emperor Emeritus), and this practice continued throughout the Heisei era. Following the accession of Emperor Naruhito, the "Imperial Address" was reinstated during the 70th festival in 2019 (Reiwa 1) in Aichi Prefecture.<ref>{{Cite news |author=Ayako Nakata |url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM5X5DNLM5XUTIL04S.html |title=Imperial Address Resumes at National Tree-Planting Festival After 11 Years |newspaper=Asahi Shimbun |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO45589990S9A600C1000000/ |title=Full Text of Emperor’s Address at National Tree-Planting Festival |newspaper=Nikkei |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref> [[Category:ppp]]

[[Category:National Tree-Planting Festival]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:National Tree-Planting Festival]][[Category:qq]] <!--National Tree-Planting Festival-->[[Category:National Tree-Planting Festival]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:National Tree-Planting Festival]][[Category:qq]] <!--National Tree-Planting Festival-->[[Category:National Tree-Planting Festival]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:National Tree-Planting Festival]][[Category:qq]] <!--National Tree-Planting Festival-->[[Category:Recurring events established in 1950]] [[Category:National Tree-Planting Festival]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:National Tree-Planting Festival]][[Category:qq]] <!--National Tree-Planting Festival-->[[Category:National Tree-Planting Festival]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:National Tree-Planting Festival]][[Category:qq]] <!--National Tree-Planting Festival-->[[Category:National Tree-Planting Festival]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:National Tree-Planting Festival]][[Category:qq]] <!--National Tree-Planting Festival-->[[Category:q--q]] [[sn:National Tree-Planting Festival]][[Category:qq]][[sn:National Tree-Planting Festival]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:全国植樹祭]] '''National Tree-Planting Festival''' (全国植樹祭, Zenkoku Shokujusai) is a Japanese tree-planting festival. As a central event of the {{ill|National Land Afforestation Campaign|ja|国土緑化運動}}, it has been held annually in spring since 1950 (Shōwa 25).
== Overview == [[File:Saitobaru Onodatesho 2004.JPG|thumb|Venue for the 55th festival at {{ill|Saitobaru Burial Mounds|ja|西都原古墳群}}, featuring the "Onodate Place"]] The festival is organized by the {{ill|National Land Afforestation Promotion Organization|ja|国土緑化推進機構}} and the host prefecture (rotating among all prefectures). The president of the event is traditionally the Speaker of the House of Representatives.
During the main ceremony, the Emperor delivers an "Imperial Address" (おことば), participates in the ceremonial tree planting and seed sowing alongside the Empress, and attends memorial tree planting events by participants from within and outside the host prefecture. The ceremony also features awards for contests such as the National Land Afforestation Poster Competition and culminates with a festival declaration. The event is recognized as one of the "Four Major Imperial Visits," along with the {{ill|National Sports Festival|ja|国民スポーツ大会}}, {{ill|National Festival of the Sea|ja|全国豊かな海づくり大会}}, and the {{ill|National Cultural Festival|ja|国民文化祭}}. Highlights of the event are later broadcast in an edited format by NHK.
The festival traces its roots to the "Forest Day Planting Ceremony" held before World War II. The Emperor's involvement began with a commemorative tree-planting ceremony held in 1949 (Shōwa 24) at {{ill|Sengokuhara|ja|仙石原}} in Hakone Town, Kanagawa Prefecture.<ref>{{Cite book |language=ja |author=Takeshi Hara |title=Complete Record of Emperor Shōwa's Imperial Train Journeys |publisher=Shinchōsha |year=2016 |page=101 |isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |language=ja |title=Fifteen Years of the National Land Afforestation Movement |year=1965 |publisher=National Land Afforestation Promotion Committee |page=110 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/2506203/1/63}}</ref> The event evolved into its current form starting with the "Tree-Planting Ceremony and National Land Afforestation Festival" held in Yamanashi Prefecture in 1950 (Shōwa 25).
Initially scheduled in the first week of April to coincide with "Green Week,"<ref>“Their Majesties Attend Tree-Planting Festival,” *Asahi Shimbun,* March 31, 1951</ref> the festival has increasingly been held in May since the 11th event in 1960 (Shōwa 35).
In 1970 (Shōwa 45), during the 21st festival in Fukushima Prefecture, the event adopted its current name. From 1977 (Shōwa 52), starting with the 28th festival in Wakayama Prefecture, the {{ill|National Tree-Care Festival|ja|全国育樹祭}} has been held in autumn to prune and care for trees planted during previous festivals, with the Crown Prince attending these events.
At its peak, the festival drew over 10,000 participants. For instance, the venue in Kaneyama Town, Yamagata Prefecture, in 2002 (Heisei 14) attracted 12,000 attendees. However, since the 2000s, financial difficulties faced by local governments and challenges in setting up large venues have led to a decline in scale, with the 2013 (Heisei 25) venue in Nanbu Town, Tottori Prefecture, accommodating around 7,000 people, including staff.<ref>[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/656620/jissigaiyou1.pdf Overview of the 2013 National Tree-Planting Festival (Tottori Prefecture website)]</ref>
Starting with the 60th festival in 2009 (Heisei 21) in Fukui Prefecture, the Emperor's "Imperial Address" was omitted as part of efforts to reduce the public duties of Emperor Akihito (current Emperor Emeritus), and this practice continued throughout the Heisei era. Following the accession of Emperor Naruhito, the "Imperial Address" was reinstated during the 70th festival in 2019 (Reiwa 1) in Aichi Prefecture.<ref>{{Cite news |author=Ayako Nakata |url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM5X5DNLM5XUTIL04S.html |title=Imperial Address Resumes at National Tree-Planting Festival After 11 Years |newspaper=Asahi Shimbun |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO45589990S9A600C1000000/ |title=Full Text of Emperor’s Address at National Tree-Planting Festival |newspaper=Nikkei |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref> [[Category:ppp]]

Highlights of the event are later broadcast in an edited format by NHK.

The Emperor's involvement began with a commemorative tree-planting ceremony held in 1949 (Shōwa 24) at {{ill|Sengokuhara|ja|仙石原}} in Hakone Town, Kanagawa Prefecture.<ref>{{Cite book |language=ja |author=Takeshi Hara |title=Complete Record of Emperor Shōwa's Imperial Train Journeys |publisher=Shinchōsha |year=2016 |page=101 |isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |language=ja |title=Fifteen Years of the National Land Afforestation Movement |year=1965 |publisher=National Land Afforestation Promotion Committee |page=110 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/2506203/1/63}}</ref> The event evolved into its current form starting with the "Tree-Planting Ceremony and National Land Afforestation Festival" held in Yamanashi Prefecture in 1950 (Shōwa 25).
Initially scheduled in the first week of April to coincide with "Green Week,"<ref>“Their Majesties Attend Tree-Planting Festival,” *Asahi Shimbun,* March 31, 1951</ref> the festival has increasingly been held in May since the 11th event in 1960 (Shōwa 35).
In 1970 (Shōwa 45), during the 21st festival in Fukushima Prefecture, the event adopted its current name. From 1977 (Shōwa 52), starting with the 28th festival in Wakayama Prefecture, the {{ill|National Tree-Care Festival|ja|全国育樹祭}} has been held in autumn to prune and care for trees planted during previous festivals, with the Crown Prince attending these events.
At its peak, the festival drew over 10,000 participants. For instance, the venue in Kaneyama Town, Yamagata Prefecture, in 2002 (Heisei 14) attracted 12,000 attendees. However, since the 2000s, financial difficulties faced by local governments and challenges in setting up large venues have led to a decline in scale, with the 2013 (Heisei 25) venue in Nanbu Town, Tottori Prefecture, accommodating around 7,000 people, including staff.<ref>[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/656620/jissigaiyou1.pdf Overview of the 2013 National Tree-Planting Festival (Tottori Prefecture website)]</ref>
Starting with the 60th festival in 2009 (Heisei 21) in Fukui Prefecture, the Emperor's "Imperial Address" was omitted as part of efforts to reduce the public duties of Emperor Akihito (current Emperor Emeritus), and this practice continued throughout the Heisei era. Following the accession of Emperor Naruhito, the "Imperial Address" was reinstated during the 70th festival in 2019 (Reiwa 1) in Aichi Prefecture.<ref>{{Cite news |author=Ayako Nakata |url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM5X5DNLM5XUTIL04S.html |title=Imperial Address Resumes at National Tree-Planting Festival After 11 Years |newspaper=Asahi Shimbun |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO45589990S9A600C1000000/ |title=Full Text of Emperor’s Address at National Tree-Planting Festival |newspaper=Nikkei |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref> [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]]

The ceremony also features awards for contests such as the National Land Afforestation Poster Competition and culminates with a festival declaration. The event is recognized as one of the "Four Major Imperial Visits," along with the {{ill|National Sports Festival|ja|国民スポーツ大会}}, {{ill|National Festival of the Sea|ja|全国豊かな海づくり大会}}, and the {{ill|National Cultural Festival|ja|国民文化祭}}. The Emperor's involvement began with a commemorative tree-planting ceremony held in 1949 (Shōwa 24) at {{ill|Sengokuhara|ja|仙石原}} in Hakone Town, Kanagawa Prefecture.<ref>{{Cite book |language=ja |author=Takeshi Hara |title=Complete Record of Emperor Shōwa's Imperial Train Journeys |publisher=Shinchōsha |year=2016 |page=101 |isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |language=ja |title=Fifteen Years of the National Land Afforestation Movement |year=1965 |publisher=National Land Afforestation Promotion Committee |page=110 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/2506203/1/63}}</ref> The event evolved into its current form starting with the "Tree-Planting Ceremony and National Land Afforestation Festival" held in Yamanashi Prefecture in 1950 (Shōwa 25).
Initially scheduled in the first week of April to coincide with "Green Week,"<ref>“Their Majesties Attend Tree-Planting Festival,” *Asahi Shimbun,* March 31, 1951</ref> the festival has increasingly been held in May since the 11th event in 1960 (Shōwa 35).
In 1970 (Shōwa 45), during the 21st festival in Fukushima Prefecture, the event adopted its current name. From 1977 (Shōwa 52), starting with the 28th festival in Wakayama Prefecture, the {{ill|National Tree-Care Festival|ja|全国育樹祭}} has been held in autumn to prune and care for trees planted during previous festivals, with the Crown Prince attending these events.
At its peak, the festival drew over 10,000 participants. For instance, the venue in Kaneyama Town, Yamagata Prefecture, in 2002 (Heisei 14) attracted 12,000 attendees. However, since the 2000s, financial difficulties faced by local governments and challenges in setting up large venues have led to a decline in scale, with the 2013 (Heisei 25) venue in Nanbu Town, Tottori Prefecture, accommodating around 7,000 people, including staff.<ref>[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/656620/jissigaiyou1.pdf Overview of the 2013 National Tree-Planting Festival (Tottori Prefecture website)]</ref>
Starting with the 60th festival in 2009 (Heisei 21) in Fukui Prefecture, the Emperor's "Imperial Address" was omitted as part of efforts to reduce the public duties of Emperor Akihito (current Emperor Emeritus), and this practice continued throughout the Heisei era. Following the accession of Emperor Naruhito, the "Imperial Address" was reinstated during the 70th festival in 2019 (Reiwa 1) in Aichi Prefecture.<ref>{{Cite news |author=Ayako Nakata |url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM5X5DNLM5XUTIL04S.html |title=Imperial Address Resumes at National Tree-Planting Festival After 11 Years |newspaper=Asahi Shimbun |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO45589990S9A600C1000000/ |title=Full Text of Emperor’s Address at National Tree-Planting Festival |newspaper=Nikkei |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref> [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:National Tree-Planting Festival]][[Category:qq]] {{DEFAULTSORT:National Tree-Planting Festival}}

[[sn:National Tree-Planting Festival]][[Category:qq]][[sn:National Tree-Planting Festival]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:全国植樹祭]] '''National Tree-Planting Festival''' (全国植樹祭, Zenkoku Shokujusai) is a Japanese tree-planting festival. As a central event of the {{ill|National Land Afforestation Campaign|ja|国土緑化運動}}, it has been held annually in spring since 1950 (Shōwa 25).
== Overview == [[File:Saitobaru Onodatesho 2004.JPG|thumb|Venue for the 55th festival at {{ill|Saitobaru Burial Mounds|ja|西都原古墳群}}, featuring the "Onodate Place"]] The festival is organized by the {{ill|National Land Afforestation Promotion Organization|ja|国土緑化推進機構}} and the host prefecture (rotating among all prefectures). The president of the event is traditionally the Speaker of the House of Representatives.
During the main ceremony, the Emperor delivers an "Imperial Address" (おことば), participates in the ceremonial tree planting and seed sowing alongside the Empress, and attends memorial tree planting events by participants from within and outside the host prefecture. The ceremony also features awards for contests such as the National Land Afforestation Poster Competition and culminates with a festival declaration. The event is recognized as one of the "Four Major Imperial Visits," along with the {{ill|National Sports Festival|ja|国民スポーツ大会}}, {{ill|National Festival of the Sea|ja|全国豊かな海づくり大会}}, and the {{ill|National Cultural Festival|ja|国民文化祭}}. Highlights of the event are later broadcast in an edited format by NHK.
The festival traces its roots to the "Forest Day Planting Ceremony" held before World War II. The Emperor's involvement began with a commemorative tree-planting ceremony held in 1949 (Shōwa 24) at {{ill|Sengokuhara|ja|仙石原}} in Hakone Town, Kanagawa Prefecture.<ref>{{Cite book |language=ja |author=Takeshi Hara |title=Complete Record of Emperor Shōwa's Imperial Train Journeys |publisher=Shinchōsha |year=2016 |page=101 |isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |language=ja |title=Fifteen Years of the National Land Afforestation Movement |year=1965 |publisher=National Land Afforestation Promotion Committee |page=110 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/2506203/1/63}}</ref> The event evolved into its current form starting with the "Tree-Planting Ceremony and National Land Afforestation Festival" held in Yamanashi Prefecture in 1950 (Shōwa 25).
Initially scheduled in the first week of April to coincide with "Green Week,"<ref>“Their Majesties Attend Tree-Planting Festival,” *Asahi Shimbun,* March 31, 1951</ref> the festival has increasingly been held in May since the 11th event in 1960 (Shōwa 35).
In 1970 (Shōwa 45), during the 21st festival in Fukushima Prefecture, the event adopted its current name. From 1977 (Shōwa 52), starting with the 28th festival in Wakayama Prefecture, the {{ill|National Tree-Care Festival|ja|全国育樹祭}} has been held in autumn to prune and care for trees planted during previous festivals, with the Crown Prince attending these events.
At its peak, the festival drew over 10,000 participants. For instance, the venue in Kaneyama Town, Yamagata Prefecture, in 2002 (Heisei 14) attracted 12,000 attendees. However, since the 2000s, financial difficulties faced by local governments and challenges in setting up large venues have led to a decline in scale, with the 2013 (Heisei 25) venue in Nanbu Town, Tottori Prefecture, accommodating around 7,000 people, including staff.<ref>[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/656620/jissigaiyou1.pdf Overview of the 2013 National Tree-Planting Festival (Tottori Prefecture website)]</ref>
Starting with the 60th festival in 2009 (Heisei 21) in Fukui Prefecture, the Emperor's "Imperial Address" was omitted as part of efforts to reduce the public duties of Emperor Akihito (current Emperor Emeritus), and this practice continued throughout the Heisei era. Following the accession of Emperor Naruhito, the "Imperial Address" was reinstated during the 70th festival in 2019 (Reiwa 1) in Aichi Prefecture.<ref>{{Cite news |author=Ayako Nakata |url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM5X5DNLM5XUTIL04S.html |title=Imperial Address Resumes at National Tree-Planting Festival After 11 Years |newspaper=Asahi Shimbun |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO45589990S9A600C1000000/ |title=Full Text of Emperor’s Address at National Tree-Planting Festival |newspaper=Nikkei |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref> [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:National Tree-Planting Festival]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:National Tree-Planting Festival]][[Category:qq]] {{DEFAULTSORT:National Tree-Planting Festival}}

[[ja:全国植樹祭]] '''National Tree-Planting Festival''' (全国植樹祭, Zenkoku Shokujusai) is a Japanese tree-planting festival. As a central event of the {{ill|National Land Afforestation Campaign|ja|国土緑化運動}}, it has been held annually in spring since 1950 (Shōwa 25).
== Overview == [[File:Saitobaru Onodatesho 2004.JPG|thumb|Venue for the 55th festival at {{ill|Saitobaru Burial Mounds|ja|西都原古墳群}}, featuring the "Onodate Place"]] The festival is organized by the {{ill|National Land Afforestation Promotion Organization|ja|国土緑化推進機構}} and the host prefecture (rotating among all prefectures). The president of the event is traditionally the Speaker of the House of Representatives.
During the main ceremony, the Emperor delivers an "Imperial Address" (おことば), participates in the ceremonial tree planting and seed sowing alongside the Empress, and attends memorial tree planting events by participants from within and outside the host prefecture. The ceremony also features awards for contests such as the National Land Afforestation Poster Competition and culminates with a festival declaration. The event is recognized as one of the "Four Major Imperial Visits," along with the {{ill|National Sports Festival|ja|国民スポーツ大会}}, {{ill|National Festival of the Sea|ja|全国豊かな海づくり大会}}, and the {{ill|National Cultural Festival|ja|国民文化祭}}. Highlights of the event are later broadcast in an edited format by NHK.
The festival traces its roots to the "Forest Day Planting Ceremony" held before World War II. The Emperor's involvement began with a commemorative tree-planting ceremony held in 1949 (Shōwa 24) at {{ill|Sengokuhara|ja|仙石原}} in Hakone Town, Kanagawa Prefecture.<ref>{{Cite book |language=ja |author=Takeshi Hara |title=Complete Record of Emperor Shōwa's Imperial Train Journeys |publisher=Shinchōsha |year=2016 |page=101 |isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |language=ja |title=Fifteen Years of the National Land Afforestation Movement |year=1965 |publisher=National Land Afforestation Promotion Committee |page=110 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/2506203/1/63}}</ref> The event evolved into its current form starting with the "Tree-Planting Ceremony and National Land Afforestation Festival" held in Yamanashi Prefecture in 1950 (Shōwa 25).
Initially scheduled in the first week of April to coincide with "Green Week,"<ref>“Their Majesties Attend Tree-Planting Festival,” *Asahi Shimbun,* March 31, 1951</ref> the festival has increasingly been held in May since the 11th event in 1960 (Shōwa 35).
In 1970 (Shōwa 45), during the 21st festival in Fukushima Prefecture, the event adopted its current name. From 1977 (Shōwa 52), starting with the 28th festival in Wakayama Prefecture, the {{ill|National Tree-Care Festival|ja|全国育樹祭}} has been held in autumn to prune and care for trees planted during previous festivals, with the Crown Prince attending these events.
At its peak, the festival drew over 10,000 participants. For instance, the venue in Kaneyama Town, Yamagata Prefecture, in 2002 (Heisei 14) attracted 12,000 attendees. However, since the 2000s, financial difficulties faced by local governments and challenges in setting up large venues have led to a decline in scale, with the 2013 (Heisei 25) venue in Nanbu Town, Tottori Prefecture, accommodating around 7,000 people, including staff.<ref>[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/656620/jissigaiyou1.pdf Overview of the 2013 National Tree-Planting Festival (Tottori Prefecture website)]</ref>
Starting with the 60th festival in 2009 (Heisei 21) in Fukui Prefecture, the Emperor's "Imperial Address" was omitted as part of efforts to reduce the public duties of Emperor Akihito (current Emperor Emeritus), and this practice continued throughout the Heisei era. Following the accession of Emperor Naruhito, the "Imperial Address" was reinstated during the 70th festival in 2019 (Reiwa 1) in Aichi Prefecture.<ref>{{Cite news |author=Ayako Nakata |url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM5X5DNLM5XUTIL04S.html |title=Imperial Address Resumes at National Tree-Planting Festival After 11 Years |newspaper=Asahi Shimbun |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO45589990S9A600C1000000/ |title=Full Text of Emperor’s Address at National Tree-Planting Festival |newspaper=Nikkei |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref> [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:National Tree-Planting Festival]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:National Tree-Planting Festival]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:National Tree-Planting Festival]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:National Tree-Planting Festival]][[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:National Tree-Planting Festival}}[[Category:qq]] {{DEFAULTSORT:National Tree-Planting Festival}}

Tree-Planting Festival]][[Category:11]] <!--National Tree-Planting Festival-->[[Category:11]] [[sn:National Tree-Planting Festival]][[Category:11]][[sn:National Tree-Planting Festival]][[Category:11]] [[ja:全国植樹祭]] '''National Tree-Planting Festival''' (全国植樹祭, Zenkoku Shokujusai) is a Japanese tree-planting festival. As a central event of the {{ill|National Land Afforestation Campaign|ja|国土緑化運動}}, it has been held annually in spring since 1950 (Shōwa 25).
== Overview == [[File:Saitobaru Onodatesho 2004.JPG|thumb|Venue for the 55th festival at {{ill|Saitobaru Kofuns|ja|西都原古墳群}}, featuring the "Onodate Place"]] The festival is organized by the {{ill|National Land Afforestation Promotion Organization|ja|国土緑化推進機構}} and the host prefecture (rotating among all prefectures). The president of the event is traditionally the Speaker of the House of Representatives.
During the main ceremony, the Emperor delivers an "Imperial Address" (おことば), participates in the ceremonial tree planting and seed sowing alongside the Empress, and attends memorial tree planting events by participants from within and outside the host prefecture. The ceremony also features awards for contests such as the National Land Afforestation Poster Competition and culminates with a festival declaration. The event is recognized as one of the "Four Major Imperial Visits," along with the {{ill|National Sports Festival|ja|国民スポーツ大会}}, {{ill|National Festival of the Sea|ja|全国豊かな海づくり大会}}, and the {{ill|National Cultural Festival|ja|国民文化祭}}. Highlights of the event are later broadcast in an edited format by NHK.
The festival traces its roots to the "Forest Day Planting Ceremony" held before World War II. The Emperor's involvement began with a commemorative tree-planting ceremony held in 1949 (Shōwa 24) at {{ill|Sengokuhara|ja|仙石原}} in Hakone Town, Kanagawa Prefecture.<ref>{{Cite book |language=ja |author=Takeshi Hara |title=Complete Record of Emperor Shōwa's Imperial Train Journeys |publisher=Shinchōsha |year=2016 |page=101 |isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |language=ja |title=Fifteen Years of the National Land Afforestation Movement |year=1965 |publisher=National Land Afforestation Promotion Committee |page=110 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/2506203/1/63}}</ref> The event evolved into its current form starting with the "Tree-Planting Ceremony and National Land Afforestation Festival" held in Yamanashi Prefecture in 1950 (Shōwa 25).
Initially scheduled in the first week of April to coincide with "Green Week,"<ref>“Their Majesties Attend Tree-Planting Festival,” *Asahi Shimbun,* March 31, 1951</ref> the festival has increasingly been held in May since the 11th event in 1960 (Shōwa 35).
In 1970 (Shōwa 45), during the 21st festival in Fukushima Prefecture, the event adopted its current name. From 1977 (Shōwa 52), starting with the 28th festival in Wakayama Prefecture, the {{ill|National Tree-Care Festival|ja|全国育樹祭}} has been held in autumn to prune and care for trees planted during previous festivals, with the Crown Prince attending these events.
At its peak, the festival drew over 10,000 participants. For instance, the venue in Kaneyama Town, Yamagata Prefecture, in 2002 (Heisei 14) attracted 12,000 attendees. However, since the 2000s, financial difficulties faced by local governments and challenges in setting up large venues have led to a decline in scale, with the 2013 (Heisei 25) venue in Nanbu Town, Tottori Prefecture, accommodating around 7,000 people, including staff.<ref>[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/656620/jissigaiyou1.pdf Overview of the 2013 National Tree-Planting Festival (Tottori Prefecture website)]</ref>
Starting with the 60th festival in 2009 (Heisei 21) in Fukui Prefecture, the Emperor's "Imperial Address" was omitted as part of efforts to reduce the public duties of Emperor Akihito (current Emperor Emeritus), and this practice continued throughout the Heisei era. Following the accession of Emperor Naruhito, the "Imperial Address" was reinstated during the 70th festival in 2019 (Reiwa 1) in Aichi Prefecture.<ref>{{Cite news |author=Ayako Nakata |url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM5X5DNLM5XUTIL04S.html |title=Imperial Address Resumes at National Tree-Planting Festival After 11 Years |newspaper=Asahi Shimbun |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO45589990S9A600C1000000/ |title=Full Text of Emperor’s Address at National Tree-Planting Festival |newspaper=Nikkei |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref> [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:National Tree-Planting Festival]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:National Tree-Planting Festival]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:National Tree-Planting Festival]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:National Tree-Planting Festival]][[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:National Tree-Planting Festival}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:National Tree-Planting Festival]][[Category:qq]] {{DEFAULTSORT:National Tree-Planting Festival}}

As a central event of the {{ill|National Land Afforestation Campaign|ja|国土緑化運動}}, it has been held annually in spring since 1950 (Shōwa 25).

During the main ceremony, the Emperor delivers an "Imperial Address" (おことば), participates in the ceremonial tree planting and seed sowing alongside the Empress, and attends memorial tree planting events by participants from within and outside the host prefecture. The ceremony also features awards for contests such as the National Land Afforestation Poster Competition and culminates with a festival declaration. The event is recognized as one of the "Four Major Imperial Visits," along with the {{ill|National Sports Festival|ja|国民スポーツ大会}}, {{ill|National Festival of the Sea|ja|全国豊かな海づくり大会}}, and the {{ill|National Cultural Festival|ja|国民文化祭}}. Initially scheduled in the first week of April to coincide with "Green Week,"<ref>“Their Majesties Attend Tree-Planting Festival,” *Asahi Shimbun,* March 31, 1951</ref> the festival has increasingly been held in May since the 11th event in 1960 (Shōwa 35).
