As a central event of the {{ill|National Land Afforestation Campaign|ja|国土緑化運動|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Land Afforestation Campaign|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it has been held annually in spring since 1950 (Shōwa 25).
== Overview == [[File:Saitobaru Onodatesho 2004.JPG|thumb|Venue for the 55th festival at [[:en:Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|Saitobaru Kofuns]], featuring the "Onodate Place"]] The festival is organized by the {{ill|National Land Afforestation Promotion Organization|ja|国土緑化推進機構|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Land Afforestation Promotion Organization|1=National Land Afforestation Promotion Organization|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420663}} and the host prefecture (rotating among all prefectures). The president of the event is traditionally the Speaker of the House of Representatives.
During the main ceremony, the Emperor delivers an "Imperial Address" (おことば), participates in the ceremonial tree planting and seed sowing alongside the Empress, and attends memorial tree planting events by participants from within and outside the host prefecture. The ceremony also features awards for contests such as the National Land Afforestation Poster Competition and culminates with a festival declaration. The event is recognized as one of the "Four Major Imperial Visits," along with the [[:en:National Sports Festival of Japan|National Sports Festival]], {{ill|National Festival of the Sea|ja|全国豊かな海づくり大会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Festival of the Sea|1=The National Convention for the Development of an Abundantly Productive Sea|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11389560}}, and the [[:en:National Cultural Festival|National Cultural Festival]]. Highlights of the event are later broadcast in an edited format by NHK.
The festival traces its roots to the "Forest Day Planting Ceremony" held before World War II. The Emperor's involvement began with a commemorative tree-planting ceremony held in 1949 (Shōwa 24) at [[:en:Sengokuhara|Sengokuhara]] in Hakone Town, Kanagawa Prefecture.<ref>{{Cite book |language=ja |author=Takeshi Hara |title=Complete Record of Emperor Shōwa's Imperial Train Journeys |publisher=Shinchōsha |year=2016 |page=101 |isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |language=ja |title=Fifteen Years of the National Land Afforestation Movement |year=1965 |publisher=National Land Afforestation Promotion Committee |page=110 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/2506203/1/63}}</ref> The event evolved into its current form starting with the "Tree-Planting Ceremony and National Land Afforestation Festival" held in Yamanashi Prefecture in 1950 (Shōwa 25).
Initially scheduled in the first week of April to coincide with "Green Week,"<ref>“Their Majesties Attend Tree-Planting Festival,” *Asahi Shimbun,* March 31, 1951</ref> the festival has increasingly been held in May since the 11th event in 1960 (Shōwa 35).
In 1970 (Shōwa 45), during the 21st festival in Fukushima Prefecture, the event adopted its current name. From 1977 (Shōwa 52), starting with the 28th festival in Wakayama Prefecture, the {{ill|National Tree-Care Festival|ja|全国育樹祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Tree-Care Festival|1=National Tree-Care Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11389535}} has been held in autumn to prune and care for trees planted during previous festivals, with the Crown Prince attending these events.
At its peak, the festival drew over 10,000 participants. For instance, the venue in Kaneyama Town, Yamagata Prefecture, in 2002 (Heisei 14) attracted 12,000 attendees. However, since the 2000s, financial difficulties faced by local governments and challenges in setting up large venues have led to a decline in scale, with the 2013 (Heisei 25) venue in Nanbu Town, Tottori Prefecture, accommodating around 7,000 people, including staff.<ref>[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/656620/jissigaiyou1.pdf Overview of the 2013 National Tree-Planting Festival (Tottori Prefecture website)]</ref>
Starting with the 60th festival in 2009 (Heisei 21) in Fukui Prefecture, the Emperor's "Imperial Address" was omitted as part of efforts to reduce the public duties of Emperor Akihito (current Emperor Emeritus), and this practice continued throughout the Heisei era. Following the accession of Emperor Naruhito, the "Imperial Address" was reinstated during the 70th festival in 2019 (Reiwa 1) in Aichi Prefecture.<ref>{{Cite news |author=Ayako Nakata |url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM5X5DNLM5XUTIL04S.html |title=Imperial Address Resumes at National Tree-Planting Festival After 11 Years |newspaper=Asahi Shimbun |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO45589990S9A600C1000000/ |title=Full Text of Emperor’s Address at National Tree-Planting Festival |newspaper=Nikkei |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref>

Following the accession of Emperor Naruhito, the "Imperial Address" was reinstated during the 70th festival in 2019 (Reiwa 1) in Aichi Prefecture.<ref>{{Cite news |author=Ayako Nakata |url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM5X5DNLM5XUTIL04S.html |title=Imperial Address Resumes at National Tree-Planting Festival After 11 Years |newspaper=Asahi Shimbun |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO45589990S9A600C1000000/ |title=Full Text of Emperor’s Address at National Tree-Planting Festival |newspaper=Nikkei |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:全国植樹祭]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11389414]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''National Tree-Planting Festival''' (全国植樹祭, Zenkoku Shokujusai) is a Japanese tree-planting festival. As a central event of the {{ill|National Land Afforestation Campaign|ja|国土緑化運動|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Land Afforestation Campaign|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it has been held annually in spring since 1950 (Shōwa 25).
== Overview == [[File:Saitobaru Onodatesho 2004.JPG|thumb|Venue for the 55th festival at [[:en:Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|Saitobaru Kofuns]], featuring the "Onodate Place"]] The festival is organized by the {{ill|National Land Afforestation Promotion Organization|ja|国土緑化推進機構|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Land Afforestation Promotion Organization|1=National Land Afforestation Promotion Organization|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420663}} and the host prefecture (rotating among all prefectures). The president of the event is traditionally the Speaker of the House of Representatives.
During the main ceremony, the Emperor delivers an "Imperial Address" (おことば), participates in the ceremonial tree planting and seed sowing alongside the Empress, and attends memorial tree planting events by participants from within and outside the host prefecture. The ceremony also features awards for contests such as the National Land Afforestation Poster Competition and culminates with a festival declaration. The event is recognized as one of the "Four Major Imperial Visits," along with the [[:en:National Sports Festival of Japan|National Sports Festival]], {{ill|National Festival of the Sea|ja|全国豊かな海づくり大会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Festival of the Sea|1=The National Convention for the Development of an Abundantly Productive Sea|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11389560}}, and the [[:en:National Cultural Festival|National Cultural Festival]]. Highlights of the event are later broadcast in an edited format by NHK.
The festival traces its roots to the "Forest Day Planting Ceremony" held before World War II. The Emperor's involvement began with a commemorative tree-planting ceremony held in 1949 (Shōwa 24) at [[:en:Sengokuhara|Sengokuhara]] in Hakone Town, Kanagawa Prefecture.<ref>{{Cite book |language=ja |author=Takeshi Hara |title=Complete Record of Emperor Shōwa's Imperial Train Journeys |publisher=Shinchōsha |year=2016 |page=101 |isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |language=ja |title=Fifteen Years of the National Land Afforestation Movement |year=1965 |publisher=National Land Afforestation Promotion Committee |page=110 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/2506203/1/63}}</ref> The event evolved into its current form starting with the "Tree-Planting Ceremony and National Land Afforestation Festival" held in Yamanashi Prefecture in 1950 (Shōwa 25).
Initially scheduled in the first week of April to coincide with "Green Week,"<ref>“Their Majesties Attend Tree-Planting Festival,” *Asahi Shimbun,* March 31, 1951</ref> the festival has increasingly been held in May since the 11th event in 1960 (Shōwa 35).
In 1970 (Shōwa 45), during the 21st festival in Fukushima Prefecture, the event adopted its current name. From 1977 (Shōwa 52), starting with the 28th festival in Wakayama Prefecture, the {{ill|National Tree-Care Festival|ja|全国育樹祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Tree-Care Festival|1=National Tree-Care Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11389535}} has been held in autumn to prune and care for trees planted during previous festivals, with the Crown Prince attending these events.
At its peak, the festival drew over 10,000 participants. For instance, the venue in Kaneyama Town, Yamagata Prefecture, in 2002 (Heisei 14) attracted 12,000 attendees. However, since the 2000s, financial difficulties faced by local governments and challenges in setting up large venues have led to a decline in scale, with the 2013 (Heisei 25) venue in Nanbu Town, Tottori Prefecture, accommodating around 7,000 people, including staff.<ref>[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/656620/jissigaiyou1.pdf Overview of the 2013 National Tree-Planting Festival (Tottori Prefecture website)]</ref>
Starting with the 60th festival in 2009 (Heisei 21) in Fukui Prefecture, the Emperor's "Imperial Address" was omitted as part of efforts to reduce the public duties of Emperor Akihito (current Emperor Emeritus), and this practice continued throughout the Heisei era. Following the accession of Emperor Naruhito, the "Imperial Address" was reinstated during the 70th festival in 2019 (Reiwa 1) in Aichi Prefecture.<ref>{{Cite news |author=Ayako Nakata |url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM5X5DNLM5XUTIL04S.html |title=Imperial Address Resumes at National Tree-Planting Festival After 11 Years |newspaper=Asahi Shimbun |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO45589990S9A600C1000000/ |title=Full Text of Emperor’s Address at National Tree-Planting Festival |newspaper=Nikkei |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref>

During the main ceremony, the Emperor delivers an "Imperial Address" (おことば), participates in the ceremonial tree planting and seed sowing alongside the Empress, and attends memorial tree planting events by participants from within and outside the host prefecture. The ceremony also features awards for contests such as the National Land Afforestation Poster Competition and culminates with a festival declaration. The event is recognized as one of the "Four Major Imperial Visits," along with the [[:en:National Sports Festival of Japan|National Sports Festival]], {{ill|National Festival of the Sea|ja|全国豊かな海づくり大会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Festival of the Sea|1=The National Convention for the Development of an Abundantly Productive Sea|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11389560}}, and the [[:en:National Cultural Festival|National Cultural Festival]]. Highlights of the event are later broadcast in an edited format by NHK.
The festival traces its roots to the "Forest Day Planting Ceremony" held before World War II. The Emperor's involvement began with a commemorative tree-planting ceremony held in 1949 (Shōwa 24) at [[:en:Sengokuhara|Sengokuhara]] in Hakone Town, Kanagawa Prefecture.<ref>{{Cite book |language=ja |author=Takeshi Hara |title=Complete Record of Emperor Shōwa's Imperial Train Journeys |publisher=Shinchōsha |year=2016 |page=101 |isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |language=ja |title=Fifteen Years of the National Land Afforestation Movement |year=1965 |publisher=National Land Afforestation Promotion Committee |page=110 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/2506203/1/63}}</ref> The event evolved into its current form starting with the "Tree-Planting Ceremony and National Land Afforestation Festival" held in Yamanashi Prefecture in 1950 (Shōwa 25).
Initially scheduled in the first week of April to coincide with "Green Week,"<ref>“Their Majesties Attend Tree-Planting Festival,” *Asahi Shimbun,* March 31, 1951</ref> the festival has increasingly been held in May since the 11th event in 1960 (Shōwa 35).
In 1970 (Shōwa 45), during the 21st festival in Fukushima Prefecture, the event adopted its current name. From 1977 (Shōwa 52), starting with the 28th festival in Wakayama Prefecture, the {{ill|National Tree-Care Festival|ja|全国育樹祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Tree-Care Festival|1=National Tree-Care Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11389535}} has been held in autumn to prune and care for trees planted during previous festivals, with the Crown Prince attending these events.
At its peak, the festival drew over 10,000 participants. For instance, the venue in Kaneyama Town, Yamagata Prefecture, in 2002 (Heisei 14) attracted 12,000 attendees. However, since the 2000s, financial difficulties faced by local governments and challenges in setting up large venues have led to a decline in scale, with the 2013 (Heisei 25) venue in Nanbu Town, Tottori Prefecture, accommodating around 7,000 people, including staff.<ref>[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/656620/jissigaiyou1.pdf Overview of the 2013 National Tree-Planting Festival (Tottori Prefecture website)]</ref>
Starting with the 60th festival in 2009 (Heisei 21) in Fukui Prefecture, the Emperor's "Imperial Address" was omitted as part of efforts to reduce the public duties of Emperor Akihito (current Emperor Emeritus), and this practice continued throughout the Heisei era. Following the accession of Emperor Naruhito, the "Imperial Address" was reinstated during the 70th festival in 2019 (Reiwa 1) in Aichi Prefecture.<ref>{{Cite news |author=Ayako Nakata |url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM5X5DNLM5XUTIL04S.html |title=Imperial Address Resumes at National Tree-Planting Festival After 11 Years |newspaper=Asahi Shimbun |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO45589990S9A600C1000000/ |title=Full Text of Emperor’s Address at National Tree-Planting Festival |newspaper=Nikkei |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref>
==References== {{translated page|ja|全国植樹祭|version=104556649|comment=Imported full ja history}}

Following the accession of Emperor Naruhito, the "Imperial Address" was reinstated during the 70th festival in 2019 (Reiwa 1) in Aichi Prefecture.<ref>{{Cite news |author=Ayako Nakata |url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM5X5DNLM5XUTIL04S.html |title=Imperial Address Resumes at National Tree-Planting Festival After 11 Years |newspaper=Asahi Shimbun |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO45589990S9A600C1000000/ |title=Full Text of Emperor’s Address at National Tree-Planting Festival |newspaper=Nikkei |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:全国植樹祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:全国植樹祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:全国植樹祭]]
'''National Tree-Planting Festival''' (全国植樹祭, Zenkoku Shokujusai) is a Japanese tree-planting festival. As a central event of the {{ill|National Land Afforestation Campaign|ja|国土緑化運動|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Land Afforestation Campaign|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it has been held annually in spring since 1950 (Shōwa 25).
== Overview == [[File:Saitobaru Onodatesho 2004.JPG|thumb|Venue for the 55th festival at {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|en|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|WD=Q3469314|lt=Saitobaru Kofuns}}, featuring the "Onodate Place"]] The festival is organized by the {{ill|National Land Afforestation Promotion Organization|ja|国土緑化推進機構|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Land Afforestation Promotion Organization|1=National Land Afforestation Promotion Organization|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420663}} and the host prefecture (rotating among all prefectures). The president of the event is traditionally the Speaker of the House of Representatives.
During the main ceremony, the Emperor delivers an "Imperial Address" (おことば), participates in the ceremonial tree planting and seed sowing alongside the Empress, and attends memorial tree planting events by participants from within and outside the host prefecture. The ceremony also features awards for contests such as the National Land Afforestation Poster Competition and culminates with a festival declaration. The event is recognized as one of the "Four Major Imperial Visits," along with the {{ill|National Sports Festival of Japan|en|National Sports Festival of Japan|WD=Q6978652|lt=National Sports Festival}}, {{ill|National Festival of the Sea|ja|全国豊かな海づくり大会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Festival of the Sea|1=The National Convention for the Development of an Abundantly Productive Sea|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11389560}}, and the {{ill|National Cultural Festival|en|National Cultural Festival|WD=Q11421126|lt=National Cultural Festival}}. Highlights of the event are later broadcast in an edited format by NHK.
The festival traces its roots to the "Forest Day Planting Ceremony" held before World War II. The Emperor's involvement began with a commemorative tree-planting ceremony held in 1949 (Shōwa 24) at {{ill|Sengokuhara|en|Sengokuhara|WD=Q11378164|lt=Sengokuhara}} in Hakone Town, Kanagawa Prefecture.<ref>{{Cite book |language=ja |author=Takeshi Hara |title=Complete Record of Emperor Shōwa's Imperial Train Journeys |publisher=Shinchōsha |year=2016 |page=101 |isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |language=ja |title=Fifteen Years of the National Land Afforestation Movement |year=1965 |publisher=National Land Afforestation Promotion Committee |page=110 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/2506203/1/63}}</ref> The event evolved into its current form starting with the "Tree-Planting Ceremony and National Land Afforestation Festival" held in Yamanashi Prefecture in 1950 (Shōwa 25).
Initially scheduled in the first week of April to coincide with "Green Week,"<ref>“Their Majesties Attend Tree-Planting Festival,” *Asahi Shimbun,* March 31, 1951</ref> the festival has increasingly been held in May since the 11th event in 1960 (Shōwa 35).
In 1970 (Shōwa 45), during the 21st festival in Fukushima Prefecture, the event adopted its current name. From 1977 (Shōwa 52), starting with the 28th festival in Wakayama Prefecture, the {{ill|National Tree-Care Festival|ja|全国育樹祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Tree-Care Festival|1=National Tree-Care Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11389535}} has been held in autumn to prune and care for trees planted during previous festivals, with the Crown Prince attending these events.
At its peak, the festival drew over 10,000 participants. For instance, the venue in Kaneyama Town, Yamagata Prefecture, in 2002 (Heisei 14) attracted 12,000 attendees. However, since the 2000s, financial difficulties faced by local governments and challenges in setting up large venues have led to a decline in scale, with the 2013 (Heisei 25) venue in Nanbu Town, Tottori Prefecture, accommodating around 7,000 people, including staff.<ref>[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/656620/jissigaiyou1.pdf Overview of the 2013 National Tree-Planting Festival (Tottori Prefecture website)]</ref>
Starting with the 60th festival in 2009 (Heisei 21) in Fukui Prefecture, the Emperor's "Imperial Address" was omitted as part of efforts to reduce the public duties of Emperor Akihito (current Emperor Emeritus), and this practice continued throughout the Heisei era. Following the accession of Emperor Naruhito, the "Imperial Address" was reinstated during the 70th festival in 2019 (Reiwa 1) in Aichi Prefecture.<ref>{{Cite news |author=Ayako Nakata |url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM5X5DNLM5XUTIL04S.html |title=Imperial Address Resumes at National Tree-Planting Festival After 11 Years |newspaper=Asahi Shimbun |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO45589990S9A600C1000000/ |title=Full Text of Emperor’s Address at National Tree-Planting Festival |newspaper=Nikkei |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref>
==References== {{translated page|ja|全国植樹祭|version=104556649|comment=Imported full ja history}}

During the main ceremony, the Emperor delivers an "Imperial Address" (おことば), participates in the ceremonial tree planting and seed sowing alongside the Empress, and attends memorial tree planting events by participants from within and outside the host prefecture. The ceremony also features awards for contests such as the National Land Afforestation Poster Competition and culminates with a festival declaration. The event is recognized as one of the "Four Major Imperial Visits," along with the {{ill|National Sports Festival of Japan|en|National Sports Festival of Japan|WD=Q6978652|lt=National Sports Festival|1=National Sports Festival of Japan}}, {{ill|National Festival of the Sea|ja|全国豊かな海づくり大会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Festival of the Sea|1=The National Convention for the Development of an Abundantly Productive Sea|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11389560}}, and the {{ill|National Cultural Festival|en|National Cultural Festival|WD=Q11421126|lt=National Cultural Festival|1=National Cultural Festival}}. Highlights of the event are later broadcast in an edited format by NHK.
The festival traces its roots to the "Forest Day Planting Ceremony" held before World War II. The Emperor's involvement began with a commemorative tree-planting ceremony held in 1949 (Shōwa 24) at {{ill|Sengokuhara|en|Sengokuhara|WD=Q11378164|lt=Sengokuhara|1=Sengokuhara}} in Hakone Town, Kanagawa Prefecture.<ref>{{Cite book |language=ja |author=Takeshi Hara |title=Complete Record of Emperor Shōwa's Imperial Train Journeys |publisher=Shinchōsha |year=2016 |page=101 |isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |language=ja |title=Fifteen Years of the National Land Afforestation Movement |year=1965 |publisher=National Land Afforestation Promotion Committee |page=110 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/2506203/1/63}}</ref> The event evolved into its current form starting with the "Tree-Planting Ceremony and National Land Afforestation Festival" held in Yamanashi Prefecture in 1950 (Shōwa 25).
Initially scheduled in the first week of April to coincide with "Green Week,"<ref>“Their Majesties Attend Tree-Planting Festival,” *Asahi Shimbun,* March 31, 1951</ref> the festival has increasingly been held in May since the 11th event in 1960 (Shōwa 35).
In 1970 (Shōwa 45), during the 21st festival in Fukushima Prefecture, the event adopted its current name. From 1977 (Shōwa 52), starting with the 28th festival in Wakayama Prefecture, the {{ill|National Tree-Care Festival|ja|全国育樹祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Tree-Care Festival|1=National Tree-Care Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11389535}} has been held in autumn to prune and care for trees planted during previous festivals, with the Crown Prince attending these events.
At its peak, the festival drew over 10,000 participants. For instance, the venue in Kaneyama Town, Yamagata Prefecture, in 2002 (Heisei 14) attracted 12,000 attendees. However, since the 2000s, financial difficulties faced by local governments and challenges in setting up large venues have led to a decline in scale, with the 2013 (Heisei 25) venue in Nanbu Town, Tottori Prefecture, accommodating around 7,000 people, including staff.<ref>[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/656620/jissigaiyou1.pdf Overview of the 2013 National Tree-Planting Festival (Tottori Prefecture website)]</ref>
Starting with the 60th festival in 2009 (Heisei 21) in Fukui Prefecture, the Emperor's "Imperial Address" was omitted as part of efforts to reduce the public duties of Emperor Akihito (current Emperor Emeritus), and this practice continued throughout the Heisei era. Following the accession of Emperor Naruhito, the "Imperial Address" was reinstated during the 70th festival in 2019 (Reiwa 1) in Aichi Prefecture.<ref>{{Cite news |author=Ayako Nakata |url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM5X5DNLM5XUTIL04S.html |title=Imperial Address Resumes at National Tree-Planting Festival After 11 Years |newspaper=Asahi Shimbun |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO45589990S9A600C1000000/ |title=Full Text of Emperor’s Address at National Tree-Planting Festival |newspaper=Nikkei |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref>
==References== {{translated page|ja|全国植樹祭|version=104556649|comment=Imported full ja history}}

revision.text:
'''National Tree-Planting Festival''' (全国植樹祭, Zenkoku Shokujusai) is a Japanese tree-planting festival. As a central event of the {{ill|National Land Afforestation Campaign|ja|国土緑化運動|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Land Afforestation Campaign|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it has been held annually in spring since 1950 (Shōwa 25).
== Overview == [[File:Saitobaru Onodatesho 2004.JPG|thumb|Venue for the 55th festival at {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|en|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|WD=Q3469314|lt=Saitobaru Kofuns|1=Saitobaru Kofun Cluster}}, featuring the "Onodate Place"]] The festival is organized by the {{ill|National Land Afforestation Promotion Organization|ja|国土緑化推進機構|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Land Afforestation Promotion Organization|1=National Land Afforestation Promotion Organization|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420663}} and the host prefecture (rotating among all prefectures). The president of the event is traditionally the Speaker of the House of Representatives.
During the main ceremony, the Emperor delivers an "Imperial Address" (おことば), participates in the ceremonial tree planting and seed sowing alongside the Empress, and attends memorial tree planting events by participants from within and outside the host prefecture. The ceremony also features awards for contests such as the National Land Afforestation Poster Competition and culminates with a festival declaration. The event is recognized as one of the "Four Major Imperial Visits," along with the {{ill|National Sports Festival of Japan|en|National Sports Festival of Japan|WD=Q6978652|lt=National Sports Festival|1=National Sports Festival of Japan}}, {{ill|National Festival of the Sea|ja|全国豊かな海づくり大会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Festival of the Sea|1=The National Convention for the Development of an Abundantly Productive Sea|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11389560}}, and the {{ill|National Cultural Festival|en|National Cultural Festival|WD=Q11421126|lt=National Cultural Festival|1=National Cultural Festival}}. Highlights of the event are later broadcast in an edited format by NHK.
The festival traces its roots to the "Forest Day Planting Ceremony" held before World War II. The Emperor's involvement began with a commemorative tree-planting ceremony held in 1949 (Shōwa 24) at {{ill|Sengokuhara|en|Sengokuhara|WD=Q11378164|lt=Sengokuhara|1=Sengokuhara}} in Hakone Town, Kanagawa Prefecture.<ref>{{Cite book |language=ja |author=Takeshi Hara |title=Complete Record of Emperor Shōwa's Imperial Train Journeys |publisher=Shinchōsha |year=2016 |page=101 |isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |language=ja |title=Fifteen Years of the National Land Afforestation Movement |year=1965 |publisher=National Land Afforestation Promotion Committee |page=110 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/2506203/1/63}}</ref> The event evolved into its current form starting with the "Tree-Planting Ceremony and National Land Afforestation Festival" held in Yamanashi Prefecture in 1950 (Shōwa 25).
Initially scheduled in the first week of April to coincide with "Green Week,"<ref>“Their Majesties Attend Tree-Planting Festival,” *Asahi Shimbun,* March 31, 1951</ref> the festival has increasingly been held in May since the 11th event in 1960 (Shōwa 35).
In 1970 (Shōwa 45), during the 21st festival in Fukushima Prefecture, the event adopted its current name. From 1977 (Shōwa 52), starting with the 28th festival in Wakayama Prefecture, the {{ill|National Tree-Care Festival|ja|全国育樹祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Tree-Care Festival|1=National Tree-Care Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11389535}} has been held in autumn to prune and care for trees planted during previous festivals, with the Crown Prince attending these events.
At its peak, the festival drew over 10,000 participants. For instance, the venue in Kaneyama Town, Yamagata Prefecture, in 2002 (Heisei 14) attracted 12,000 attendees. However, since the 2000s, financial difficulties faced by local governments and challenges in setting up large venues have led to a decline in scale, with the 2013 (Heisei 25) venue in Nanbu Town, Tottori Prefecture, accommodating around 7,000 people, including staff.<ref>[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/656620/jissigaiyou1.pdf Overview of the 2013 National Tree-Planting Festival (Tottori Prefecture website)]</ref>
Starting with the 60th festival in 2009 (Heisei 21) in Fukui Prefecture, the Emperor's "Imperial Address" was omitted as part of efforts to reduce the public duties of Emperor Akihito (current Emperor Emeritus), and this practice continued throughout the Heisei era. Following the accession of Emperor Naruhito, the "Imperial Address" was reinstated during the 70th festival in 2019 (Reiwa 1) in Aichi Prefecture.<ref>{{Cite news |author=Ayako Nakata |url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASM5X5DNLM5XUTIL04S.html |title=Imperial Address Resumes at National Tree-Planting Festival After 11 Years |newspaper=Asahi Shimbun |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO45589990S9A600C1000000/ |title=Full Text of Emperor’s Address at National Tree-Planting Festival |newspaper=Nikkei |date=June 2, 2019 |accessdate=June 2, 2019}}</ref>
